column3: What percent of non-digitally mature healthcare providers do not use digital technologies to personalize care as per the survey?
column13:
Is the Healthcare Industry Digitally Fit?
Our Survey Findings

Consumers are taking charge of their health using digital tools

45% search for health information on social media channels

4 million mobile health app downloads occur/ day

Only 33% of healthcare providers are digitally mature

A comparison of Healthcare Providers by Digital Maturity

Digitally Mature Healthcare Providers            Non-Digitally Mature Healthcare Providers

Use of Social Media Channels

58% use social media to offer services to customers

18% use social media to offer services to customers

Use of Mobile Channels

63% use mobile channels to offer services to customers

13% use mobile channels to offer services to customers

Use of Digital Technologies to Personalize Care

47% use digital technologies to personalize care

21% use digital technologies to personalize care

Approach to Digital Transformation

79% share a common vision towards digital transformation across senior management

84% allocate adequate funding to digital transformation

28% share a common vision towards digital transformation across senior management

31% allocate adequate funding to digital transformation

Process Digitization

63% have automated core processes

31% have automated core processes

Integrated Views of Data

84% have an integrated view of customer data

33% have an integrated view of customer data

Investments in Digital Skills

84% invest in building digital skills

36% invest in building digital skills

How Can Healthcare Providers Move Up the Digital Curve?

Define a Vision and Secure Top-Management Buy-in

Define transformation goals based on patient needs

Assess existing digital initiatives, processes, systems and digital skill levels

Establish a Transformation Roadmap and Governance Model to Drive Digital Initiatives

Identify quick wins and long-term digital initiatives

Set up a dedicated Digital Services Unit (DSU)

Promote Internal Collaboration and Knowledge Sharing to Drive Internal Engagement

Build a digital communication backbone through online training and knowledge sharing platforms

Leverage early-adopters of digital to help evangelize digital technologies internally

Prioritize Skill Development and Operational Excellence for a Sustained Digital Advantage

Invest in training programs and hire experienced digital professionals

Streamline operations and develop a single source of truth for customer, operations and financial data

Sources:
1. National Research Corporation, "Consumers Going Digital With Their Healthcare Experiences Means Providers Must Step It Up", March 2014
2. BusinessInsider, "10 Ways Mobile Is Transforming Health Care", June 2014
3. Capgemini Consulting Analysis

SANITATION MARKETING
UNSATISFIED SANITATION NEEDS

MARKET DRIVERS

WHAT ARE CONSTRAINTS TO INVESTMENT?

UNMET CUSTOMER NEEDS
"Why should I pay for a toilet or latrine?"
"Why should I settle if I can't afford the dream?"

The poor are challenging customers who would rather make do with their current sanitation situation if they cannot find a desirable, affordable product in their local market.

WEAK SUPPLY CHAIN
"How do I reach poor households?"
"Why should I prioritize sanitation in my portfolio?"

The sanitation market is highly fragmented and uncoordinated, making it difficult to develop and deliver products that consumers are willing and able to buy. Enterprises with the scale to tackle these industry challenges do not currently see sanitation or the poor as priority markets; businesses that do focus on sanitation have limited reach and product differentiation.

DIFFICULT INVESTMENT CLIMATE
"How can we improve the ease of doing business in the sanitation sector?"
"Why should we invest in this even riskier business?"

Current sanitation policies are not focused on lifting barriers to market entry. Despite the existence of government sanitation promotion programs, sanitation enterprises do not have enough information about viable markets and lack sufficient access to finance.

KEY CHALLENGES

1
Stimulate Demand
- Show consumers that good sanitation is a smart economic decision
- Design sanitation solutions that meet poor customers' needs and preferences
- Offer flexible financing options

2
Strengthen the Sanitation Supply Chain
- Build business capacity and market intelligence
- Encourage formation of strategic partnerships to develop stronger distribution channels to reach remote customers

3
Improve the Investment Climate
- Communicate clear policies that promote improved sanitation
- Facilitate private sector provision by clearly defining the role of the private sector in government strategy and policy

RECOMMENDATIONS

For more information, visit www.wsp.org/dpsp

* All dollar amounts provided are in US dollars
* Some figures have been rounded; please see the full publication for more details
* Enterprise data from in-country surveys (n=300–700). See full report for details.

WHAT ARE CONSTRAINTS TO INVESTMENT?

UNMET CUSTOMER NEEDS

"Why should I pay for a toilet or latrine?"
"Why should I settle if I can't afford the dream?"

The poor are challenging customers who would rather make do with their current sanitation situation if they cannot find a desirable, affordable product in their local market.

WEAK SUPPLY CHAIN

"How do I reach poor households?"
"Why should I prioritize sanitation in my portfolio?"

The sanitation market is highly fragmented and uncoordinated, making it difficult to develop and deliver products that consumers are willing and able to buy. Enterprises with the scale to tackle these industry challenges do not currently see sanitation or the poor as priority markets; businesses that do focus on sanitation have limited reach and product differentiation.

DIFFICULT INVESTMENT CLIMATE

"How can we improve the ease of doing business in the sanitation sector?"
"Why should we invest in this even riskier business?"

Current sanitation policies are not focused on lifting barriers to market entry. Despite the existence of government sanitation promotion programs, sanitation enterprises do not have enough information about viable markets and lack sufficient access to finance.

KEY CHALLENGES

RECOMMENDATIONS

1 Stimulate Demand
- Show consumers that good sanitation is a smart economic decision
- Design sanitation solutions that meet poor customers' needs and preferences
- Offer flexible financing options

2 Strengthen the Sanitation Supply Chain
- Build business capacity and market intelligence
- Encourage formation of strategic partnerships to develop stronger distribution channels to reach remote customers

3 Improve the Investment Climate
- Communicate clear policies that promote improved sanitation
- Facilitate private sector provision by clearly defining the role of the private sector in government strategy and policy

For more information, visit www.wsp.org/dpsp

* Some figures have been rounded. Please see the full publication for more details.
* Original data taken from country surveys - see endnotes.

column3: What is the cost of a cup of coffee in Luanda and Tokyo, taken together?
column13:
TOP 10 MOST EXPENSIVE CITIES TO LIVE IN 2010

1. LUANDA
Angola
Prices for housing and food continue to rise dramatically in Luanda, making this city the most expensive place to live in for 2010.

The city has experienced an oil boom, which has meant Luanda grew well beyond its means, very quickly, and attracted waves of foreign workers - all competing for the now scarce resources.

When we estimated the two thirds of Angolans live on less than $2 a day, most will never be able to afford a car or even their kitchen, even though the oil boom has made gasoline the most affordable commodity where one litre of gas costs just $0.55. To rent a luxury two bedroom apartment will cost $7,000 per month and even a fast food meal will cost you $17.50.

2. TOKYO
Japan
Tokyo is one of the most populated cities in the world, and as the capital of Japan, Tokyo now has more than 13 million people living in the 2,000 square kilometres of the city. As a result, housing in a penthouse in central Tokyo is often hit by earthquakes, and can be costly to maintain because of insurance. It also boasts some of the world's most expensive land is Tokyo.

Expats often need to live in the city, and as a result can find themselves paying $4,436 a month for a luxury two bedroom apartment.

3. N'DJAMENA
Chad
N'Djamena has a high cost of living due to years of civil war, which has made foreign workers reluctant to invest any capital into the region. The infrastructure and local markets have been ravaged by the civil unrest and food is in short supply. The remoteness of the region and high transport costs have helped make prices high for those products which can be brought in. In the end services are expensive, with the average cost of the most basic items being higher than in the world's wealthy regions such as Geneva and Tokyo. In N'Djamena one of the poorest in the world also, where 80% of the population live below the poverty line.

4. MOSCOW
Russia
In Moscow, the energy, financial, software development and engineering industries attract a large and wealthy expatriate community. This means that prices for real estate, retail and services have been pushed significantly higher.

This new found wealth has meant that salaries have also inflated many luxury retailers. Moscow has seen an increase in luxury hotels and restaurants, but even a $3,600 per month rent on a luxury 2 bed apartment is not uncommon. In the centre of Moscow, a coffee can cost $8.29 making this the most expensive city for a cup of coffee in this list.

5. GENEVA
Switzerland
Geneva is a truly global city, the head of the United Nations and the Red Cross, and home to numerous financial institutions. Geneva is a global city and the centre for diplomacy and the international community. In Geneva food prices are 45% more expensive than anywhere else in western Europe and Switzerland also has some of the world's most expensive rent and apartments in Geneva are expensive as well. The city is home to the global elite and as such costs reflect this. A two bedroom luxury apartment starting at $4,010 per month.

6. OSAKA
Japan
On the same island as Tokyo, Osaka also has a high population density of approximately 2 million people. There are also a number of large manufacturers, and the local economy is built around heavy industry and transport. The central business district is also a major retail and business hub and as a result, rents and items for everyday use such as coffee are expensive.

7. LIBREVILLE
Gabon
The capital, Libreville lies in a coastal city and as a result is the country's largest city, and a major port and trade centre for lumber. The desire for expatriates to continue to consume western foods and goods makes Libreville very expensive to live in. Many of the items and goods for local consumption are imported and as a result the cost of living is high. Fast food meals can be expensive and local groceries and fresh foods are costly. For most of us, will cost $27.08.

8. HONG KONG
Tied in 8th place, Hong Kong is an expensive hub of trade and finance with a highly developed infrastructure and services and particularly real estate, where housing is at a premium. Hong Kong has the highest population density of any major city and as such space is at a premium. With 1,104 square kilometres, and is home to more than 6.8 million people, living in Hong Kong is expensive. With $4,248 per month, a luxury two bedroom apartment in Hong Kong will cost around $4,248 per month.

9. ZURICH
Switzerland
Zurich is the commercial capital of Switzerland - fulfilling its role as a major business centre, and the headquarters for many foreign corporations. Zurich is the most expensive city in Switzerland and this attracts some of the top minds from around the world.

Zurich has the largest number of company bankruptcies in 2009 according to corelation agency Dun & Bradstreet. The inflation rate has also been increasing for 2010 after a dip the previous year, and the city has a very strong economy.

10. COPENHAGEN
Denmark
Copenhagen is an expensive place to live due to the strong wage rates and high taxation. The city's high quality of life, with state welfare and worker protections, combined with high prices for goods and services make Copenhagen expensive. Quality of life in Copenhagen is one of the highest in the world, which reflects its high cost of living.

Copenhagen is home to the world's largest wind turbine manufacturer, producing much of the world's wind turbines. The cost of a luxury two bedroom apartment in Copenhagen is $1,94.

column3: How many states shared the border during the American Civil War?

column15: Five. The traditional "border states" were Delaware, Maryland, Kentucky, and Missouri (West Virginia, created in 1863, is sometimes counted as a fifth or sixth depending on the source).

column3: What percentage of business analysts work in manufacturing, finance, and insurance, taken together?
column13:
BUSINESS ANALYTICS vs DATA SCIENCE

In the age of big data, parsing unwieldy amounts of information can lead to world-changing innovation.
To understand this data, companies are hiring a variety of specialists, including business analysts and data scientists.

Who Are They?

BUSINESS ANALYSTS
Research and extract valuable information from structured and unstructured sources to explain historical, current, and future business performance; determine the best analytical models and approaches to present and explain solutions to business users.

DATA SCIENTISTS
Design, develop, and deploy algorithms through statistical programming that support business decision-making tools; manage large amounts of data; create visualizations to aid in understanding.

What Education Do They Have?

The majority of business analysts come from a variety of backgrounds including business and humanities, while data scientists tend to come from computer science, mathematics, and technology backgrounds.

BUSINESS ANALYSTS
30% Bachelor's
56% Master's
11% Doctoral

DATA SCIENTISTS
28% Bachelor's
48% Master's
20% Doctoral

Sample Undergraduate Majors

Business Analysts
• Business Administration
• Information Technology
• Finance
• Political Science
• Anthropology
• Economics
• History
• Psychology

Data Scientists
• Mathematics
• Computer Science
• Information Science
• Computer Software Engineering
• Technical Communications
• Computer Information Systems
• Statistics

What Skills Do They Have?

Both business analysts and data scientists are experts in using data to inform decisions, but they apply their skills in different ways, using the same or similar tools. The skills listed below describe someone who has earned a master's degree in the field.

Decision-Making
Business Analysts
• Instigate change by transforming business insights from interdisciplinary data analysis into tangible resources for business decision-making.

Data Scientists
• Discover the opportunity in datasets by mining data and writing machine learning algorithms to support decision-making processes.

Applied Problem Solving
Business Analysts
• Define business problems and translate statistical analysis into data-driven business intelligence that improves business performance.

Data Scientists
• Create the foundation of analysis by which an applied business problem can be solved.

Data Analysis
Business Analysts
• Research, interpret, and visualize raw data using predictive, prescriptive and descriptive analysis to make it useful and accessible for business users.

Data Scientists
• Manage and organize large sets of multivariate data using methods like linear discriminant analysis and multilinear regression selection.

Analytical Models
Business Analysts
• Understand, integrate, and prescribe solutions that use data modeling.

Data Scientists
• Experienced statistical programmers using languages and tools such as SAS, SQL, R, SPSS, Python, and Knime.

Database Management
Business Analysts
• Define and align database requirements for various forms of data, coded and not coded, using tools like Teradata, Oracle, and Hadoop.

Data Scientists
• Design and structure databases using tools like Teradata, Oracle, and Hadoop.

Where Do They Work?

BUSINESS ANALYSTS
25% Finance and insurance
10% Computer systems design and related services
8% Manufacturing
8% State and local government, excluding education and hospitals
8% Management of companies and enterprises

DATA SCIENTISTS
26% Federal government

13% Colleges, universities, and professional schools
11% Research and development in the physical, engineering, and life sciences
8% Software publishers

26,700

column3: which touch screen is more durable - resistive or infrared?
column5: Infrared is more durable. Resistive screens have only fair durability (prone to scratches and wear), while infrared screens are rated as good durability (glass only shatters on major impact).
column13:
HOW DOES A TOUCHSCREEN PHONE WORK?

TECHNOLOGY

There are three types of technologies widely used in touch screen phones today: resistive, capacitive, and infrared.

RESISTIVE

CAPACITIVE

INFRARED
(HEAT-SENSITIVE AND OPTICAL)

PHONES

Samsung Messager Touch, Samsung Instinct, HTC Touch Diamond, LG Dare

Huawei Ascend, Sanyo Zio, Apple's iPhone, HTC Hero, DROID Eris, Palm Pre, Blackberry Storm

Samsung U600 (heat), Neonode N2 (optical)

HOW IT WORKS

Small dots separate multiple layers of material that are transmitting currents. When the top flexible layer is pushed into the lower layer, the electrical current changes and the location of impact is calculated.

The current is broadcast from the corners of the screen. When a finger touches the screen, it changes the current, and that location is calculated.

HEAT-SENSITIVE

Heat-sensitive must be touched with a warm object to register point of impact.

OPTICAL

Optical uses sensors to shoot a grid of invisible beams just above the screen. Point of impact is calculated based upon the point where the x-y axis is interrupted.

COST TO MAKE

$

$$

$$$

SCREEN MATERIAL

A layer of flexible material (usually polyester film) over a layer of glass.

Glass

Glass

TOOL FOR INPUT

Bare fingers, gloved fingers, or a stylus

Bare fingers only

Optical: Bare fingers, gloved fingers, or a stylus

Heat-Sensitive: Warm, bare fingers

OUTDOOR VISIBILITY

Poor visibility

Good visibility
call

Good visibility, but strong ambient light impacts productivity and precision.
call

MULTI-TOUCH CAPABILITIES?

No

Yes

Yes

DURABILITY

Fair Durability. Screens are vulnerable to scratches and other minor damage. The screen will wear over time and need replacing.

Good Durability. Glass shatters upon major impact only.

Good Durability. Glass shatters upon major impact only.

SOURCES:
http://www.phonearena.com/htmls/Article-Touchscreen-technologies-in-phones-article-a_3067-p_2.html
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Touchscreen
http://www.pixartouch.com
http://www.msnbc.msn.com/id/28128280
column15: Infrared (optical) touchscreens are generally more durable. Unlike resistive screens, which rely on a flexible top membrane that wears, scratches or punctures over time, infrared systems detect touch with a non-contact light grid and typically use a solid glass surface—making them more resistant to wear, scratching and heavy use. (Resistive panels are cheaper and work with gloves/styluses, but they don’t last as long under high‑use or harsh conditions.)

Data Scientist Study

The explosion in digital data, bandwidth and processing power — combined with new tools for analyzing the data — has sparked massive interest in the emerging field of data science. Organizations of all sizes are turning to people who are capable of translating this trove of data — created by mobile sensors, social media, surveillance, medical imaging, smart grids and the like — into predictive insights that lead to business value. Despite the growing opportunity, demand for data scientists has outpaced supply of talent, and will for the next five years.

column3: How many people allowed in non-work gatherings in step 2?
column5: Up to 20 people.
column13:
Australian Government

COVIDSAFE

3 STEP FRAMEWORK FOR A COVIDSAFE AUSTRALIA

MAINTAIN 1.5M DISTANCING AND GOOD HYGIENE • STAY HOME IF UNWELL • FREQUENTLY CLEAN AND DISINFECT COMMUNAL AREAS • COVIDSAFE PLAN FOR WORKPLACES AND PREMISES

ALL STEPS ARE SUBJECT TO EXPERT HEALTH ADVICE • States and territories can implement changes based on their COVID-19 conditions

GATHERINGS & WORK
EDUCATION & CHILDCARE
RETAIL & SALES
CAFES & RESTAURANTS
ENTERTAINMENT & AMUSEMENT VENUES
SPORT & RECREATION
ACCOMMODATION
WEDDINGS, FUNERALS & RELIGIOUS SERVICES
HAIR & BEAUTY SERVICES
DOMESTIC TRAVEL

STEP 1

STEP 1: The important first small steps - connect with friends and family - allowing groups of people to be together in homes and in the community. Businesses reopen, and more people return to work

GATHERINGS & WORK (STEP 1)
Non-work gatherings of up to 10
Up to 5 visitors or more in addition to normal residents
Work from home if it works for you and/or your employer
Workplaces develop a COVIDSafe plan
Avoid public transport in peak hour

EDUCATION & CHILDCARE (STEP 1)
Child care centres, primary and secondary schools open as per state and territory plans
Universities / technical colleges to increase face-to-face where possible and prioritise hands-on, skills based learning

RETAIL & SALES (STEP 1)
Retail stores open
Retail stores and shopping centre managers must develop COVIDSafe plans
Auctions/open homes can have gatherings of up to 10, recording contact details

CAFES & RESTAURANTS (STEP 1)
May open and seat up to 10 patrons at one time
Need to maintain an average density of 4m2 per person
Food courts or areas to remain closed to seated patrons

ENTERTAINMENT & AMUSEMENT VENUES (STEP 1)
To remain closed:
Indoor movie theatres, concert venues, live music venues, museums, zoos, pubs, registered and licensed clubs, nightclubs, gambling venues, strip clubs and brothels
Exceptions: Rectangular or codes in these venues may seat up to 10 patrons at one time

SPORT & RECREATION (STEP 1)
No indoor physical activity including gyms
Community centres, outdoor gyms, playgrounds and skate parks allowed up to 10 people
Outdoor sport (up to 10 people) consistent with the AIS Framework for Rebooting Sport
Pools open with restrictions
Continue current arrangements for caravan parks and camping grounds (closed to tourists in some states and territories)
Hostels and hotels are open for accommodation

ACCOMMODATION (STEP 1)
Continue current arrangements for caravan parks and camping grounds (closed to tourists in some states and territories)
Hostels and hotels are open for accommodation

WEDDINGS, FUNERALS & RELIGIOUS SERVICES (STEP 1)
Weddings may have up to 10 guests in addition to the couple and the celebrant
Funerals may have up to 20 mourners indoors and 30 outdoors
Religious gatherings may have up to 10 attendees
Every gathering must record contact details

HAIR & BEAUTY SERVICES (STEP 1)
Hairdressers and barber shops open and record contact details
Beauty therapy and massage therapy venues, saunas and tattoo parlours remain closed

DOMESTIC TRAVEL (STEP 1)
Allow local and regional travel for recreation
Refer to state and territory governments for border restrictions and biosecurity conditions

STEP 2

STEP 2: Building on slightly larger gatherings and more businesses reopening. Higher risk activities may have tighter restrictions

GATHERINGS & WORK (STEP 2)
Non-work gatherings of up to 20
States and territories may allow larger numbers in some parts of the state
Work from home if it works for you and/or your employer
Workplaces develop a COVIDSafe plan
Avoid public transport in peak hour

EDUCATION & CHILDCARE (STEP 2)
Child care centres, primary and secondary schools open as per state and territory plans
Universities/technical colleges to increase face-to-face where possible and prioritise hands-on, skills based learning

RETAIL & SALES (STEP 2)
Retail stores open
Retail stores and shopping centre managers must develop COVIDSafe plans
Auctions/open homes can have gatherings of up to 20, recording contact details

CAFES & RESTAURANTS (STEP 2)
Cafes and restaurants can seat up to 20 patrons at one time
Need to maintain an average density of 4m2 per person
Food courts and similar closed areas to remain closed to seated patrons

ENTERTAINMENT & AMUSEMENT VENUES (STEP 2)
Indoor movie theatres, concert venues, live music venues, museums, zoos, pubs, registered and licensed clubs, nightclubs, gambling venues, strip clubs and brothels remain closed
Exceptions: Restaurants may allow seated patrons up to 20 patrons at one time

SPORT & RECREATION (STEP 2)
Up to 20 people allowed to participate in outdoor sport consistent with the AIS Framework for Rebooting Sport
Up to 20 people allowed to participate in all outdoor sports, including training
Need to maintain an average density of 4m2 per person
Pools open with restrictions

ACCOMMODATION (STEP 2)
Caravan parks and camping grounds fully open
All accommodation areas open and allow gatherings of up to 20 people

WEDDINGS, FUNERALS & RELIGIOUS SERVICES (STEP 2)
Weddings may have up to 20 guests in addition to the couple and the celebrant
Funerals may have up to 50 mourners indoors and 100 outdoors
Religious gatherings may have up to 20 attendees
Every gathering must record contact details

HAIR & BEAUTY SERVICES (STEP 2)
Hairdressers and barber shops open and record contact details
Beauty therapy and massage therapy venues can return to operation with up to 20 clients in the premises and record contact details
Saunas and tattoo parlours remain closed

DOMESTIC TRAVEL (STEP 2)
Allow local and regional travel for recreation
Consider allowing interstate recreational travel depending on the state and territory risks and border controls
Refer to state and territory governments for biosecurity conditions

STEP 3

STEP 3: A commitment to reopening of business and the community with minimal restrictions, but underpinned by COVIDSafe ways of living

GATHERINGS & WORK (STEP 3)
Non-work gatherings of up to 100 people
Larger gatherings to be considered
Return to workplace
Workplaces develop a COVIDSafe plan
Avoid public transport in peak hour

EDUCATION & CHILDCARE (STEP 3)
Child care centres, primary and secondary schools open as per state and territory plans
Universities/technical colleges to increase face-to-face where possible and prioritise hands-on, skills based learning
Consider reopening residential colleges and international student travel

RETAIL & SALES (STEP 3)
Retail stores open
Retail stores and shopping centre managers must develop COVIDSafe plans
Auctions/open homes can have gatherings of up to 100, recording contact details

CAFES & RESTAURANTS (STEP 3)
Cafes, restaurants and food courts can seat up to 100 people at one time
Need to maintain an average density of 4m2 per person

ENTERTAINMENT & AMUSEMENT VENUES (STEP 3)
Venues open in Step 2 to 00 patrons up to 100 patrons (text blurred) Consideration will be given to opening larger venues and gaming rooms
Restaurants or cafes in these venues may seat up to 100 patrons at one time
To remain closed: strip clubs and brothels

SPORT & RECREATION (STEP 3)
All venues allowed gatherings of up to 100 people
Need to maintain an average density of 4m2 per person
Community sport expansion to be considered consistent with the AIS Framework for Rebooting Sport

ACCOMMODATION (STEP 3)
All accommodation areas open and allow gatherings of up to 100 people

WEDDINGS, FUNERALS & RELIGIOUS SERVICES (STEP 3)
Allow gatherings of up to 100 people
Every gathering must record contact details

HAIR & BEAUTY SERVICES (STEP 3)
All establishments open up to 100 people
Record contact details

DOMESTIC TRAVEL (STEP 3)
Allow interstate travel
Refer to state and territory governments for biosecurity conditions

[End of infographic]

column5: California
column13:
AMERICA'S GUN VIOLENCE EPIDEMIC

Although gun violence in America is frequently attributed to the availability of firearms, another relevant factor is our nation's broken mental health care system. Although most people who suffer from mental health disorders are nonviolent, severe mental health disorders that go untreated can increase the risk for violence. Thus, if we hope to curb gun violence, we must de-stigmatize and treat mental health disorders.

GUN VIOLENCE IN AMERICA

2/3 OF ALL MASS KILLINGS INVOLVE GUNS.

In America, a mass killing — defined as having 4 victims, not including the killer — occurs once every 2 weeks.

There were 154 mass killings from 2006 to 2010.

THOSE INCLUDE
774 VICTIMS

OF THOSE VICTIMS,
161 WERE CHILDREN

GUNS & SUICIDE

31,000 GUN RELATED DEATHS IN THE UNITED STATES

19,000 OF THOSE ARE SELF INFLICTED

5% OF ALL SUICIDE ATTEMPTS ARE BY GUNSHOT.

85% OF THOSE ATTEMPTS RESULTED IN DEATH.

Suicide is the 11th leading cause of death in the United States and the third-leading cause of death for people ages 10-24 years.

In July 2007, a nationwide report indicated that male veterans are twice as likely to die by suicide as compared to their civilian peers.

ACCESS TO GUNS
FEDERAL GUN LAWS CURRENTLY ENACTED INCLUDE:

- Prohibition of firearm sales or transfers to those suffering from mental health disorders and those adjudicated mentally ill.
- Prohibition of firearm possession in schools, unless licensed by that school or the firearm is unloaded and in a locked container.
- Federal background checks on firearms purchases from licensed dealers; records of which must legally be destroyed after 24 hours.
- Prohibition of possession of fully automatic weapons by civilians.
- Prohibition of children to possess a handgun or ammunition, with exceptions for employment, practice, education and home defense.

BUT GUN CONTROL IS LARGELY DICTATED BY INDIVIDUAL STATES, CREATING LARGE DISCREPANCIES BETWEEN STATES. FOR EXAMPLE, SOUTH DAKOTA HAS SOME OF THE WEAKEST GUN LAWS AND CALIFORNIA HAS SOME OF THE STRONGEST.

SOUTH DAKOTA
- No laws restricting the reporting of mental health information to the National Instant Criminal Background Check System.
- No civil commitment information required.
- No firearm registration.
- No magazine capacity restriction.
- Handgun open carry permitted and concealed carry allowed.

CALIFORNIA
- Only state to track and disarm violent offenders and those deemed a danger to themselves or others.
- State laws require universal background checks on all firearm transfers.
- Waiting periods for handgun purchases.
- Assault weapons ban in effect.
- Limitations on magazine capacity.
- Stronger mental health reporting requirements.

Gun control laws are further weakened by discrepancies in regulations for licensed firearms dealers, which are stricter, and regulations for private sales, which are more lax.

MENTAL HEALTH AND GUN VIOLENCE

7 MILLION
Americans suffer from severe mental health issues, such as schizophrenia, acute depression or bipolar disorder.

The increased risk of violence for most mentally ill people is minimal, but when paired with issues like substance abuse and life stressors, the risk is more pronounced.

HALF OF U.S. ADULTS WILL DEVELOP A MENTAL ILLNESS DURING THEIR LIFETIME.

People with severe mental illness are roughly twice as likely to commit violent acts in their lifetime.

THE FLOUNDERING MENTAL HEALTH SYSTEM

LESS THAN HALF OF AMERICANS WITH SEVERE MENTAL HEALTH ISSUES RECEIVE ADEQUATE TREATMENT.

$1.6 BILLION in state mental health services have been cut since 2009.

These cuts led to significant reductions in hospital and community services for those with serious mental illness, including cutbacks to:
HOUSING
OUTPATIENT INTERVENTIONS
MEDICATION
CRISIS SERVICES

13% of children ages 8 to 15 live with severe mental health disorders.

BUT ONLY 20% of those children receive treatment.

ONE-HALF OF ALL LIFETIME CASES OF MENTAL ILLNESS BEGIN BY AGE 14. YET THERE ARE LONG DELAYS — SOMETIMES DECADES — BETWEEN THE FIRST ONSET OF SYMPTOMS AND WHEN PEOPLE SEEK AND RECEIVE TREATMENT.

500,000 TOTAL BEDS AVAILABLE IN AMERICAN MENTAL INSTITUTIONS AT PEAK LEVELS IN THE 1950s

43,000 TOTAL BEDS CURRENTLY AVAILABLE

This shortage of inpatient psychiatric care makes finding appropriate care difficult, if not impossible.

IN 2011
24 STATES SLASHED MENTAL HEALTH PROVIDER REIMBURSEMENT RATES FOR MEDICAID — THE MOST IMPORTANT SOURCE OF FUNDING OF PUBLIC MENTAL HEALTH SERVICES.

REAL SOLUTIONS

“We must not only tackle gun control but we must also fix deficiencies in the U.S. mental health system, gaps that can leave many people with serious mental illness and family members without an easy way to get the treatment and support we know can help avert catastrophes.”

Lynn R. Goldman, pediatrician and dean of the George Washington University School of Public Health and Health Services

New Assault Weapons Ban
Senator Dianne Feinstein has authored the Assault Weapons Ban of 2013, which would ban the sale and transfer of semi-automatic assault weapons and large-capacity magazines.

Funding Mental Health Care
The recently passed Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act will expand Medicaid coverage to more Americans to access mental health services and require mental health care coverage as an essential health benefit.

Gun Violence Research
On March 1, President Obama proposed increasing funding for gun violence research to inform evidence-based policies and prevention measures.

Destigmatizing Mental Illness
On February 4, the Department of Health & Human Services launched a national public education campaign to encourage earlier detection and treatment of mental illness, reduce stigma, and connect people to care.

Data Scientist Study

The explosion in digital data, bandwidth and processing power — combined with new tools for analyzing the data — has sparked massive interest in the emerging field of data science. Organizations of all sizes are turning to people who are capable of translating this trove of data — created by mobile sensors, social media, surveillance, medical imaging, smart grids and the like — into predictive insights that lead to business value. Despite the growing opportunity, demand for data scientists has outpaced supply of talent, and will for the next five years.

column3: When was lithography invented?
column13:
Digitec presents
A brief history of printing

WOODBLOCKS
Stamps carved into blocks of wood.
~200

How To Use Woodblocks

(illustration sequence)

PRINTING PRESS
~1440
The Gutenberg machine - A European invention

For the first time in history the Bible was printed.
A Gutenberg Press with these two components...
Movable Type
Composing Stick
...means no more hand copying

LITHOGRAPHY
1796
Based on the insolubility of oil and water, it leaves your fingers greasy.

A modern form of litho: Offset machines

(three flask icons)
Use grease-based ink to draw the image
Treat the stone with gum arabic and acid
Wet the stone before printing - ink only sticks to the greasy parts

(1) (2)

ROTARY PRESS
1843
The longest, fastest, biggest and earliest printing machine to date

The first newspaper to use it was The Baltimore Sun.

(illustration of press and rollers)

OFFSET PRINTING
1875
Colours are separated and then merged - the quality achievable with this printer is outstanding.

Colours are separated into Cyan, Magenta, Yellow and Black

(The few days to 'compose' the layout)
(The press can print extremely large runs)
(Some offset presses can even print straight onto cardboard)

SCREEN PRINTING
1910
One of the cheapest printing techniques, it could transfer a design onto virtually anything.

Screen printing is fairly easy, here is what you need: (2)

A hand* design
A silk-screen mesh
Waterbased ink
Squeegee

XEROGRAPHY
1938
copy, copy, copy, copy, copy, copy, copy, copy, copy, copy, copy, copy...

(illustration of xerographic copier)

INKJET PRINTING
1951
The computer can link in and do the first digital printers

To enjoy an inkjet printer you'll need (2)

Electricity
Ink Cartridges
Ink Tanks
A computer

LASER PRINTING
1969
A light beam that 'impresses'

What's going on inside your printer?

(illustrations of process)
Negative charge is placed on the drum by the corona wire
A laser draws the image on the drum by discharging parts of its surface
Toner is attracted to the charged parts and transferred to paper
The drum unit transfers the toner to the paper
The fuser melts the toner onto the paper and makes the print permanent

3D PRINTING
1984
The design exits the boundaries of the canvas!

It uses coloured plastic filaments as ink. (2)

Many car manufacturers are testing 3D printers for prototyping their parts
The price has made the filaments and machines accessible from around $1,000

column3: How many infinity stones are there?
column5: Six. There are six Infinity Stones (Space, Mind, Reality, Power, Time, and Soul).
column13:
EVERYTHING YOU NEED TO KNOW BEFORE WATCHING

AVENGERS
INFINITY WAR

THE INFINITY STONES

SPACE
(The Tesseract)
- Blue
- Teleportation & travel through space
- Containment/portal creation
- Appeared in: Captain America: The First Avenger (V), The Avengers (VI), Thor: Ragnarok (XVII) (as part of MCU continuity)

MIND
(The Scepter / Vision's forehead)
- Yellow
- Telepathy & mind control
- Grants intelligence & life
- Appeared in: The Avengers (VI), Avengers: Age of Ultron (XI), Captain America: Civil War (XIII) (Vision)

REALITY
(The Aether)
- Red
- Alters reality & matter
- Nightmarish, fluid-like energy
- Appeared in: Thor: The Dark World (VIII)

POWER
(The Orb)
- Purple
- Massive destructive energy
- Boosts other stones & physical power
- Appeared in: Guardians of the Galaxy (X)

TIME
(Eye of Agamotto)
- Green
- Time manipulation: loops, rewinds, fast-forwards
- Worn by: Doctor Strange
- Appeared in: Doctor Strange (XIV)

SOUL
- Orange
- Controls life & soul; mysterious requirements and rules
- Heavily guarded/holds deep metaphysical rules
- Appeared in: (revealed in context of Infinity War)

MARVEL CINEMATIC UNIVERSE
- A shared universe created by Marvel Studios
- Begins with Iron Man (2008)
- Includes interconnected characters and stories across standalone and ensemble films
- Phases structure: Phase One, Phase Two, Phase Three (culminating in Avengers films)

DC EXTENDED UNIVERSE
- DC Entertainment and Warner Bros. Pictures build a shared cinematic universe for DC characters
- Includes films like Man of Steel, Batman v Superman: Dawn of Justice, Suicide Squad, Wonder Woman, Justice League

20TH CENTURY FOX
- Own film rights to X-Men & Fantastic Four characters
- Produces separate interconnected universe movies (X-Men series, Deadpool, Fantastic Four)

SONY PICTURES
- Owns motion picture rights to Spider-Man
- Reached a deal with Marvel Studios to allow Spider-Man to appear in the MCU under certain terms

UNIVERSES & HOLLYWOOD
- Legal and licensing deals dictate where characters can appear onscreen
- Studios make agreements and creative deals to share or license characters (example: Spider-Man deal between Sony & Marvel Studios)
- Cross-studio films are rare and depend on business arrangements

MCU TIMELINE (Selected films & order)
I Iron Man
II The Incredible Hulk
III Iron Man 2
IV Thor
V Captain America: The First Avenger
VI The Avengers
VII Iron Man 3
VIII Thor: The Dark World
IX Captain America: The Winter Soldier
X Guardians of the Galaxy
XI Avengers: Age of Ultron
XII Ant-Man
XIII Captain America: Civil War
XIV Doctor Strange
XV Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 2
XVI Spider-Man: Homecoming
XVII Thor: Ragnarok
XVIII Black Panther
XIX Avengers: Infinity War

SOME KEY FILMS & NOTES
- Iron Man (I) 2008 — Tony Stark becomes Iron Man; origins of MCU
- The Incredible Hulk (II) — Bruce Banner/Hulk's solo outing within MCU continuity
- Iron Man 2 (III) — Expands Tony Stark story & S.H.I.E.L.D.
- Thor (IV) — Introduces Thor, Asgard & magic-realm elements
- Captain America: The First Avenger (V) — Introduces Steve Rogers & early Infinity Stone (Tesseract)
- The Avengers (VI) — First major team-up; Tesseract & Loki; introduction to larger threats
- Iron Man 3 (VII) — Tony deals with aftermath of The Avengers
- Thor: The Dark World (VIII) — Introduces the Aether (Reality Stone)
- Captain America: The Winter Soldier (IX) — S.H.I.E.L.D. falls; Winter Soldier revelations
- Guardians of the Galaxy (X) — Introduces Power Stone (Orb) & cosmic side of MCU
- Avengers: Age of Ultron (XI) — Vision created; Mind Stone revealed inside Loki's scepter
- Ant-Man (XII) — Quantum/size manipulation; new angle on tech & heists
- Captain America: Civil War (XIII) — Avengers split; Spider-Man introduced to MCU continuity via deal; broader implications for team
- Doctor Strange (XIV) — Introduces the Time Stone (Eye of Agamotto) & mystical dimension concepts
- Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 2 (XV) — Expands Guardians' characters & cosmic relationships
- Spider-Man: Homecoming (XVI) — Spider-Man integrated into MCU high-school angle
- Thor: Ragnarok (XVII) — Major change to Thor, destruction of Asgard; Surtur; Hulk returns to Thor storyline
- Black Panther (XVIII) — Introduces Wakanda and T'Challa; important geopolitical MCU element
- Avengers: Infinity War (XIX) — Convergence of heroes to stop Thanos collecting the Infinity Stones

NOTABLE CHARACTERS & AVENGERS APPEARANCES
- Iron Man / Tony Stark
- Captain America / Steve Rogers
- Thor Odinson
- Hulk / Bruce Banner
- Black Widow / Natasha Romanoff
- Hawkeye / Clint Barton
- Vision
- Scarlet Witch / Wanda Maximoff
- Falcon / Sam Wilson
- War Machine / James Rhodes
- Spider-Man / Peter Parker
- Doctor Strange / Stephen Strange
- Black Panther / T'Challa
- Star-Lord / Peter Quill
- Gamora
- Drax
- Rocket
- Groot
- Nebula
- Mantis
- Thanos (primary Infinity Stones antagonist)
- Loki
- Nick Fury
- Maria Hill

THE "FALSE" GAUNTLET
- Reports from early set photos and props led to speculation about an early or fake Infinity Gauntlet used for filming
- The "false" gauntlet is likely a practical prop used on set for scale and interaction, with the final gauntlet effects completed in post-production
- Prop and practical effects often used to give actors a physical reference; final visual polish added later by VFX houses
- Replica gauntlets and stone props may vary in material, color, and placement depending on production needs

ADDITIONAL NOTES & CALL OUTS
- Some characters cross over between cosmic, magic, and Earth-based plots
- The Infinity Stones each have their own unique histories within the MCU timeline and have appeared in multiple films in different forms (contained in objects like the Tesseract, Aether, Orb, Scepter, Eye of Agamotto, and reportedly held by specific entities)
- The acquisition of all six Infinity Stones by a single being grants near-omnipotent power (as explored by Thanos in Infinity War)
- Cinematic cross-overs are the result of long-term planning across films and phases of the MCU

SOURCES / CREDITS
- Marvel Studios
- Walt Disney Pictures
- Production notes, on-screen film evidence, and promotional materials

(End of extracted text)

column3: What is the fifth tip?
column4: ["share stories of other donors' generosity before asking"]
column5: SHARE STORIES OF OTHER DONORS' GENEROSITY BEFORE ASKING
column13:
4
SCIENCE OF GIVING STUDIES FUNDRAISERS CAN LEARN FROM

ON TANGIBILITY

REAL WORLD EXPERIMENT WITH TANGIBLE/INTANGIBLE CHARITIES

In a 2010 study, one group of potential donors read about an organization that was inherently tangible and the other read about an organization that was inherently intangible. The responses to each organization was measured.

DONATIONS TO CHARITY

Intangible Charity
Tangible Charity

INTANGIBLE CHARITY DESCRIPTION
- NO GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS
- NO SPECIFIC MENTION OF HOW $ USED
- NO DESCRIPTION OF PROBLEM

TANGIBLE CHARITY DESCRIPTION
- GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS
- SPECIFIC ON HOW $ IS USED
- DESCRIBES PROBLEM BEING SOLVED

DONORS GAVE ALMOST THREE TIMES MORE TO THE TANGIBLE CHARITY

ON TANGIBILITY

REAL WORLD EXPERIMENT WITH TANGIBLE/INTANGIBLE PROJECTS

In this study, the charity remained the same (Oxfam) but the focus of the appeal to donors was different. One appeal was framed with very specific information on how the donation would be used and where while the other was more broad and general in its description.

DONORS GAVE ALMOST TWICE AS MUCH TO SUPPORT THE PROJECT FRAMED IN A TANGIBLE WAY

Tangible Project
Intangible Project

ON SOCIAL INFLUENCE

FIELD EXPERIMENTS IN PUBLIC RADIO

In a 2009 field experiment with a public radio station, callers were put into four groups; those receiving no social information and those who were told about another donor who made a $75, $150 or $300 donation.

Average Donations

Social Information
Control   $75   $150   $300

CITING A PRIOR DONATION OF $300 INCREASED GIVING AN AVERAGE OF 29%

ON GOAL PROXIMITY

FIELD EXPERIMENTS IN DISASTER RELIEF

In this experiment, several thousand donors were sent one of four mailings that informed the donor about a fund they could support. One group was told nothing of the progress towards the goal, another that they were at 10%, another at 66% and another at 85%.

10%   66%   85%

MORE BENEFIT
Donation rates increased when told that the fund was at 85% of the goal.

LESS BENEFIT
There was no benefit in mentioning the fund was at 10% or 66% of the goal.

SOME TIPS

CREATE REAL TANGIBLE PROJECTS FOR DONORS TO FUND

ENCOURAGE DONORS TO SHARE THEIR DONATION (AND AMOUNT) IN PUBLIC

BE SPECIFIC IN HOW DONATIONS WILL BE USED TO MAKE AN IMPACT

BUILD TOWARDS YOUR GOAL BEFORE ASKING THE PUBLIC FOR DONATIONS

SHARE STORIES OF OTHER DONORS' GENEROSITY BEFORE ASKING

THE RECAP

DONORS GAVE ALMOST THREE TIMES AS MUCH TO THE TANGIBLE CHARITY

DONORS GAVE ALMOST TWICE AS MUCH TO SUPPORT THE PROJECT FRAMED IN A TANGIBLE WAY

CITING A PRIOR DONATION OF $300 INCREASED GIVING AN AVERAGE OF 29%

DONATION RATES INCREASED WHEN TOLD THAT THE FUND WAS AT 85% OF THE GOAL

REFERENCES

All of these studies, and more, can be found in the book THE SCIENCE OF GIVING: EXPERIMENTAL APPROACHES TO THE STUDY OF CHARITY. You can purchase the book on Amazon.

INFOGRAPHIC BROUGHT TO YOU BY:
re: charity

Piktochart
make information beautiful

column3: Which audio media has the least amount of lifespan with regular use?
column5: Magnetic cassette tapes (analog magnetic tape) — they wear fastest with regular play because the tape physically contacts the heads, can shed oxide, stretch and demagnetize, and are highly sensitive to heat/humidity.
column15: Cassette tapes (magnetic tape). With regular play they suffer tape wear/stretch, oxide loss and demagnetization and thus degrade far faster than optical formats (CDs) or properly cared-for vinyl.

column13:
A 4°C WARMER WORLD
We Can and Must Avoid It

Climate change is happening — now. Turn Down the Heat, a report on the latest climate science prepared for the World Bank, notes we’re on a path to a 4°C warmer world by the end of this century. The social, economic, and environmental consequences could be devastating. Every region of the world would be affected, but the poor and most vulnerable will suffer the most. This is not inevitable, however — with sustained policy action and climate-smart solutions, warming can still be held below 2°C.

Human impact on the climate system is unequivocal.

Ice Age → Pre-industrial Times (around 1750)
Since the last Ice Age to pre-industrial times (thousands of years ago):
- -7°C (thermometer illustration)
The global mean temperature rose by 4-5°C.
The same warming is taking place from pre-industrial times to 2100 (only about 300 years).

Pre-industrial Times (around 1750) → Today
- Sea levels have risen 15-20 cm.
- The global mean temperature is 0.8°C higher.
- Ice sheets in both Greenland and the Arctic are losing mass at an accelerating rate.
- Since 2002, 13 extreme weather events like floods, heatwaves, and hurricanes have been linked to man-made change.

Today → Future
If present emission trends continue, the world is on a path to a 4°C warming.
Likely sea level rise: 0.5-1 meter.
By 2100: 4° warmer

What could a 4°C warmer world look like?
Warming is likely to have more severe impacts on the tropics.

(World map with icons and labels)
- There would be larger impacts on agriculture and ecosystems.
- Drought and aridity would likely increase in tropical developing countries.
- Flooding and rising sea-level hotspots.
- Heat stress.
- Sea level rise is likely to be 15-20% higher in the tropics than the global mean.

Corals Driven to Extinction
Current
0.8°C warmer — Threatened with increased bleaching
1-2°C warmer — Growth slows down
2-3°C warmer — Begin to dissolve
4°C warmer — Extinction possible

Amazon Rainforest Dieback
Current
0.8°C warmer — Home to rich biodiversity
2°C warmer — Forest fires could double
4°C warmer — Large sections transform into low biomass woodlands

Water Resources Threatened
2°C warmer — Mean annual river flow declines by 4-12% in the Amazon, Mississippi, and Murray-Darling Basins
4°C warmer — All above changes likely to double

What can we do?
The steps we take today can help countries transition to low carbon and climate resilient growth paths for the future.

WE MUST:
- Make economic growth green and inclusive
- Scale up affordable technology solutions
- Invest in climate-smart infrastructure

Increasing finances to fight climate change
World Bank lending in 2012:
$7.1B Mitigation
$4.6B Adaptation

Making development climate–smart

Agriculture
Kenya
First soil carbon project
Higher yields, greater resilience, and lower emissions on 450,000 hectares

Transport
Nigeria
Green urban transport in Lagos
Bus Rapid Transport (BRT) system used by ~200,000 commuters/day

Energy
Bangladesh
Solar panels for off-grid homes
1.4 million low-income rural homes have electricity

Water
China
Integrating water resource management with pollution control
20 million+ people benefit

Policy
India
Development Policy Loan
Launching transformative actions in energy, water, industry, and cities in Himachal Pradesh

Risk Analysis
Vietnam
Decision-making under uncertainty
Helping policy-makers make risk-informed choices in Ho Chi Minh

To learn more, go to climatechange.worldbank.org
Visit the World Bank on Facebook, and follow us on twitter @wbclimatechange

Source: Turn Down the Heat: Why a 4°C warmer world must be avoided

column3: How many types of Antimicrobials?
column5: Three
column13:
Using
DISINFECTING
WIPES
at home & school

Types of Antimicrobials

Sanitizers
Reduce bacterial numbers on surfaces to a safe level.

Disinfectants
Kill bacteria, fungi, and some viruses on surfaces.

Antiseptics/Drugs
Antiseptics like hand sanitizers treat or prevent diseases on living things. They are not pesticides.

Microbes
Bacteria
Fungi
Viruses
Spores*
*Most wipes do not kill spores

What is dwell time?
-The contact time (seconds or minutes) the product needs to stay on a surface to kill microbes
-The surface should be visibly wet for the entire dwell time
-Time may change based on the microbe and purpose (sanitize vs. disinfect)

Where can they be used?
-Specific locations listed only on the label
-Use sites and dwell time vary by location and purpose

Household surfaces
Medical
Public Health

What are they?
- Antimicrobials are intended to kill or slow the growth of microbes
-Disinfectant wipes are disposable for hard surfaces like counters and tables
-Surface cleaners like wipes are not intended for use on skin
- Disinfectant wipes may LOOK similar to hand wipes. Read the label! Look for phrases like "disinfecting", "antibacterial", or "kills germs, viruses, and bacteria". When in doubt, call NPIC! 800-858-7378

Use in schools and daycares
-Only adults should use cleaning wipes – children should NOT use wipes
-Keep out of reach of children
-Follow the dwell time listed on the label
-Leave wipes in the original container
-Use the right product for each situation
-Parents: ask how children are involved in classroom cleaning

These wipes are NOT for:
Food contact
Produce
Hand wipes
Baby wipes

What is antimicrobial resistance?
If similar products are used repeatedly, they may not be as effective over time. This resistance can occur naturally, but overuse or misuse of antimicrobial products increases the chances. Follow the label and dwell time to reduce the risk of resistance.

How do I minimize risk?
-Always follow the label, including use sites and dwell times
-Never mix antimicrobial or cleaning products
-You can wear gloves if you prefer, always check the label first
-Wash hands after use
-Avoid touching wet surfaces after use
-Follow first aid instructions
-Open windows or use fans
-Store wipes out of reach of children
-Removing dirt and food from surfaces before helps wipes work
-Misuse or overuse of wipes can lead to antimicrobial resistance

Where can I get more information?
npic.orst.edu
npic@ace.orst.edu
800.858.7378

Sources
Antimicrobials Topic Fact Sheet - NPIC
Daycare and School Poison Safety - NPIC and AAPCC

column3: Who is the only wicket keeper in the world who has taken a wicket?
column5: Mahendra Singh Dhoni (M.S. Dhoni)
column13:
He studied at DAV Jawahar Vidya Mandir
where he was keenly interested in football and badminton

Mahendra Singh Dhoni was born on July 7, 1981 at Ranchi, Jharkhand located in the east corner of India

He was the goalkeeper of his football team and was able to play for the district and state level. But on the insistence of his football coach, he tried out to play cricket

He entered into a local cricket club called the Commando cricket club where he played from 1995–1998

His improved batting and keeping performances led him to be selected to the Bihar Ranji Trophy squad for the 1999/00 season

In the 1997/98 season, his superb performances in the Vinoo Mankad Under-16 Championship attracted the selector's eye

He made his debut against the Assam team by scoring an unbeaten score of 68

In 2000, he was selected to the India A team which went to tour Kenya to play 3 matches. One of the matches was against Pakistan as the third team

One-Day International

He was run out for a duck but in his 5th ODI match against Pakistan, he scored 148 off 123 balls to be called a match winning player in the Indian squad

In the series he scored 362 runs at an average of 72.40 with back to back centuries against Pakistan

He won the 'Man of the Series' award for top scoring in the tournament with 346 runs

He was made the captain of the Indian T20 squad in September 2007

One-Day Internationals

He was made the captain of the ODI team in 2007 and the Test team in 2008

He immediately made impact as he led the team to win the inaugural World Cup T20 in South Africa

In 2009, he scored 1198 runs in 24 innings, an average of 70.43 to be the highest scorer of the year in ODIs

Only a few players have been wicket-keepers Captains for their team for a long time, Dhoni ranks at no.1 beating Gary Alexander by over 15 who ranks second in that list

highest scoring wicket-keeper

He's not just a batsman and a wicket keeper, but our Captain has proven himself as a bowler too. He took the wicket of Travis Dowlin of West Indies

He is the highest scoring wicket-keeper captain on the world in a Test. He's the only wicket keeper in the world who has taken a wicket

He has been awarded the Rajiv Gandhi Khel Ratna which is the highest sports honor given by the government of India in the year 2008

He advertises more than 15 products as of 2013 and is one of the most marketable players in the world

Dhoni is the successful captain that India ever had. He led India to 27 Test match victories in 60 Tests and 107 ODI wins in 194 ODIs as a captain

In June 2013, he was reported to be the 16th highest paid sportsman in the world with yearly earnings of $31 million

Win 40 T20 matches

He captained 70 T20 matches and he had 40 wins in his account

In Indian Premier League, he signed up with Chennai Super Kings in 2008, and in the last six seasons, has won the trophy twice. He's the only captain to have achieved that feat

In February of 2015, the couple welcomed a baby girl named Ziva

In 2010, Mahi married Sakshi, his childhood sweetheart

2016 IPL

Now Dhoni Captaining Rising Pune Supergiants in 2016 IPL

On 4th January, 2017, Dhoni stepped down as captain of India's limited overs teams ahead of the ODIs and T20s against England, on 6th Jan 2017 Virat Kohli became the new captain

He will definitely be remembered as one of the most successful Indian captains in the history of the sport

He took the decision in order to give the younger players a chance to lead the side to the next level

'M.S. Dhoni: The Untold Story' is a Bollywood biographical film directed by Neeraj Pandey, starring by Sushant Singh Rajput based on the life of Mahendra Singh Dhoni

The estimate earning of MS Dhoni is approximately Rs. 25 crore per annum

THE BRUTAL ORIGINS

--- The tradition of Christmas is highly regarded as a celebration of the birth of Jesus Christ. Although popular belief states that Jesus was born on December 25th, The New Testament gives no specific date or year of his birth.

--- Roman pagans first introduced the holiday of "Saturnalia" which was a week-long period of lawlessness celebrated between December 17 - 25. During this period, Roman courts were closed, and Roman authorities stated that no one could be punished for breaking many of Rome's common laws.

--- The festival began when Roman authorities chose a random victim to represent "The Lord of Misrule." At the end of the festival on December 25th, the innocent man or woman would be brutally murdered. By doing so, Romans believed they were destroying the "forces of darkness".

--- In the 4th century C.E. Christianity adopted the "Saturnalia" festival in attempts to convert large amounts of people to Christianity. The problem was - there was nothing intrinsically Christian about the festival. To fix this, Christian leaders claimed Saturnalia's final day, December 25th, was the official birth date of Jesus Christ.

...and Christmas was born.

GREAT SKIN CONDITIONER
Due to the high concentrations of antioxidant properties of some of the compounds in Guinness, it can help condition skin by protecting cells from damage. Pour a pint in the tub for a deep skin treatment.

GOOD SOURCE OF PROTEIN
Beer and Guinness contain small amounts of protein from the grains used in brewing. They are not a major source of protein but do contribute to overall intake.

GOOD FOR THE BONES & A GOOD SOURCE OF IRON
Guinness has been shown to contain small amounts of iron, which can contribute to daily iron intake. Combined with calcium from some beers, this may help bone health in moderation.

UNCLOGGING ARTERIES
Certain compounds in beer may help reduce the risk of heart disease by reducing the clotting of blood and increasing HDL cholesterol levels. Moderation is key.

MAINTAINS HEALTHY HEART
Some studies show moderate beer consumption is linked to reduced risk of heart disease due to alcohol and polyphenols. Excessive drinking negates benefits.

HIGH IN IRON
Guinness contains more iron than many light beers which can help with iron levels in the body, but it is not a substitute for iron-rich foods.

column13:
COVID-19
Coronavirus Disease 2019

BE AWARE.

COVID-19 is a new respiratory illness that was first discovered in Wuhan, China. It is transmitted from person to person.

SYMPTOMS*

COMMON SYMPTOMS
RUNNY NOSE
COUGH
SORE THROAT

SEVERE CASES
HIGH FEVER
PNEUMONIA
SEVERE RESPIRATORY DISEASE

*Symptoms may appear 1 to 12 days following exposure to the virus

Who is most at risk of becoming seriously ill?
• People over age 60
• People with pre-existing conditions such as diabetes and heart disease

How is it transmitted?
• Through close contact with an infected person
• By an infected person coughing or sneezing
• By touching contaminated objects or surfaces and then touching your mouth, nose or eyes

Currently there is no vaccine or specific treatment. We can only treat the symptoms.
Serious cases may require oxygen and ventilatory support.

PREPARE.

Make sure you get your information from a reliable source.

Wash your hands regularly with soap and water or use an alcohol-based gel.

Cover your mouth with the inside of your elbow when you cough or sneeze or use a tissue and dispose of used tissue immediately and wash your hands.

ACT.

1M
Avoid close contact with people who have flu-like symptoms.

Avoid touching your eyes, nose or mouth with unwashed hands.

Avoid sharing cups, plates or other personal items and disinfect all surfaces that are touched frequently.

If you have traveled to areas where COVID-19 is circulating or have been in contact with someone who has it and you experience fever, cough or difficulty breathing, seek medical attention immediately. Do not self-medicate.

Note: Information may change when more is known about the disease.
February 2020.

PAHO
Pan American Health Organization
World Health Organization
Americas

BE AWARE. PREPARE. ACT.
www.paho.org/coronavirus

Social Media Guidelines

for Students and Job Seekers

Social media plays a big role in young people's lives. But how much of a role should social media play when it comes to educational or professional relationships?

01 January

Only colleagues at work

Both managers and colleagues

Quick Stats

Most young professionals appear comfortable with connecting with colleagues on social networks.

36% of young employees aged 18-29 list an employer affiliation on facebook.

16 The average number of colleagues young employees are friends with on facebook.

Who Should You Friend?

Should young professionals and students add employers?

Make this decision carefully - find out if other coworkers are friends with your manager first.

Pro
Personalized networking can be an asset when furthering your career.

Cons
Some employers may be uncomfortable to connect online, as they may have more rigid ideas of friendship on social networks.

Unprofessional activity online may impact your job if seen by your boss.

Also consider:
If you receive a friend request from a superior and want to accept, first clean up your profile and limit what he can see.

You may want to wait on friending a superior until they decide to friend you first.

If you receive a friend request from a superior and don't want to accept it, either ignore it or politely explain that you try to keep your personal and professional lives separate.

Remember to never post workplace complaints online.

More Options

Don't friend coworkers or associates you don't trust.

Should students add:

It's a great way to make friends and connect outside of class.

You can connect with others for homework help if you need it.

Classmates

Some students may not add you, but don't take this personally.

Some faculty use social networks to expand on classroom discussions or to provide homework help.

Friending a teacher could lead to better learning and educational opportunities.

Faculty

Some view teacher-student interactions on social media as inappropriate.

Should young professionals add:

Can help build inter-office relationships.

Connecting online may make balancing personal life and professional life difficult.

May lead to gossip and other inter-office problems.

Coworkers

Friending competitors can show that you support and appreciate their accomplishments.

Competitors

Your competitor may learn company secrets from you.

Your current employer may think you want to leave.

Control who can see your posts and information by customizing privacy settings.

Don't make it personal if someone decides not to friend you back.

column3: which touch screen technology is more costlier to make - resistive or infrared?
column5: Infrared is more costly to make than resistive.
column13:
HOW DOES A TOUCHSCREEN PHONE WORK?

TECHNOLOGY

There are three types of technologies widely used in touch screen phones today: resistive, capacitive, and infrared.

RESISTIVE

CAPACITIVE

INFRARED (HEAT-SENSITIVE AND OPTICAL)

PHONES

Samsung Messager Touch, Samsung Instinct, HTC Touch Diamond, LG Dare

Huawei Ascend, Sanyo Zio, Apple's iPhone, HTC Hero, DROID Eris, Palm Pre, Blackberry Storm

Samsung U600 (heat), Neonode N2 (optical)

HOW IT WORKS

Small dots separate multiple layers of material that are transmitting currents. When the top flexible layer is pushed into the lower layer, the electrical current changes and the location of impact is calculated.

The current is broadcast from the corners of the screen. When a finger touches the screen, it changes the current, and that location is calculated.

HEAT-SENSITIVE
Heat-sensitive must be touched with a warm object to register point of impact.

OPTICAL
Optical uses sensors to shoot a grid of invisible beams just above the screen. Point of input is calculated based upon the point where the x-y axis is interrupted.

COST TO MAKE

$    $$    $$$

SCREEN MATERIAL

A layer of flexible material (usually polyester film) over a layer of glass.

Glass

Glass

TOOL FOR INPUT

Bare fingers, gloved fingers, or a stylus

Bare fingers only

Optical: Bare fingers, gloved fingers, or a stylus
Heat-Sensitive: Warm, bare fingers

OUTDOOR VISIBILITY

Poor visibility

Good visibility

Good visibility, but strong ambient light impacts productivity and precision.

MULTI-TOUCH CAPABILITIES?

No

Yes

Yes

DURABILITY

Fair Durability. Screens are vulnerable to scratches and other minor damage. The screen will wear over time and need replacing.

Good Durability. Glass shatters upon major impact only.

Good Durability. Glass shatters upon major impact only.

SOURCES:
http://www.phonearena.com/htmls/Article-Touchscreen-technologies-in-phones-article_id_3067-p_2.html
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Touchscreen
http://www.gizmodo.com
http://www.msnbc.msn.com/id/28192280
column15: Infrared is more costly to make. Resistive touchscreens are simpler and cheaper — just layered films with conductive coatings and straightforward assembly — while infrared systems require emitter/receiver arrays or optical sensors, a supporting frame, and calibration, which raises component and manufacturing costs. (Costs can vary with size and design, but generally infrared is pricier.)

Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) are remote-controlled aircraft that can carry cameras, sensors and weapons over enemy territory. The decade since 9/11 has seen these remote aircraft increase in prominence from speculative prototypes to America's primary counterterrorism weapon.

The largest drone...

On the Battlefield: Use of Drones in Combat

1 Oct. 7, 2001: The first armed Predator drones begin flying missions over Afghanistan less than a month after 9/11. By March 2002, Drones would begin providing close air support for troops on the ground.

2 Nov. 3, 2002: A CIA-operated Predator drone launched a Hellfire missile at a car carrying five al-Qaida members. It was the first drone assassination in what would become a decadelong campaign.

3 Dec. 23, 2002: A Predator drone fires a sidewinder missile at an Iraqi fighter plane in the No Fly Zone. This is the first instance of air-to-air combat with a drone. During the surge in 2007, drone surveillance would significantly reduce the number of roadside bomb attacks.

4 May 7, 2004: The CIA begins a covert drone bombing campaign in Pakistan's tribal regions. Over the next four years, President George Bush would order 42 more drone strikes in the region. From 2008 to 2011, President Barack Obama has ordered at least 180 strikes. Some estimates put the death toll from the campaign at close to 2,000.

5 Late 2004: The U.S. Border Patrol begins testing UAVs for patrolling the U.S./Mexico border. In 2009, the U.S. begins flying drones inside of Mexican airspace in support of Mexican government antidrug cartel operations.

6 Late 2009: U.S. begins drone flights off Somalia to monitor and deter pirates. In June 2011, the U.S. begins basing drones at the Mogadishu airport and launching drone missile strikes within Somalia against al-Shabab militants linked to al-Qaida.

7 April 21, 2011: President Obama approves the use of drones in support of the Libyan rebels. The drones destroy Qaddafi air defenses in advance of the NATO bombing campaign, and provide targeting and support for NATO allies during the war. Operations in Libya also feature the first use of drone helicopters in combat.

8 Jan. 25, 2011: A Texas Department of Public Safety SWAT team deploys a small drone during a raid on a drug stash house. This marks the first use of UAVs for domestic surveillance.

The State Of The World's Female CEOs

How Many Women?
In 2010, (the most recent year of available data) only 12 Fortune Global 500 companies are run by women. This is down from 13 in 2009.

488    12    2.4%

What about in America?:
The Standard & Poor's 500 Index is made of:
484 male CEOs and 16 female CEOs

Females CEOs make up just 3.2% of America's S&P 500.

"Catalyst research studied Fortune 500 companies in the United States and found, on average, firms with the largest number of women senior executives compared with those with the fewest numbers, had a 35% higher return on equity."

484    16    3.2%

Canada ahead of the rest:
In Canada, females hold 6.4% of all top officer positions at Financial Post 500* companies.

"Although there are 30 companies with women CEOs/heads, there are only 26 women who head those companies. [one female CEO] in particular heads four companies, [and another] heads two."

*469 companies responding

439    30    6.4%

Top 3 Earning Women:

1. Carol Bartz, the CEO of Yahoo!
$47.2 million

2. Irene Rosenfeld, CEO of Kraft
$26.3 million

3. Indra Nooyi, the CEO of Pepsi Co
$15.8 million

Irene is regarded as the second most powerful woman in the world, second only to First Lady Michelle Obama. Rosenfeld claims that being a woman "has not been the focus of most of the assessments of me or many of the other female leaders."

A Kellogg University School of Management study on woman leaders found that "as a board's gender composition evolved and increased its number of female seats, over time the likelihood that these boards would select female managers also increased."

A recent economic study published by the Wall Street Journal found that male CEOs with female daughters work to close the pay gap at their companies and pay female employees by as much 13% in some cases. Birth of either male or female children to female CEOs produced no such effect.

Earning Statistics:

Average S&P 500 Salaries:
Average $8 million    Average $14 million

For females This represents 43% more than their male counterparts.

In 2009, male CEOs' took a 5% cut.

While salaries for females CEOs' rose 19%.

S&P 500 aside, in a study of 1.1 million CEOs from companies of all sizes, revenues, etc, it was found that women CEOs only make about 75% of what male CEOs make.

Where do they come from?
"Women CEOs are almost twice as likely to have been named to the job from outside, as Cafaro was, than from within, according to a Harvard Business Review study."

Still a long way to go:
The highest paid male CEO is Gregory B. Maffei of Liberty Media Corp with a pre-tax income of $87.1 million. Compare this to Carol Bartz, the highest paid female CEO at just $47.2 million.

This means that Maffei is paid 184.5% more than Bartz.

column13:
EMPLOYEE SOCIAL MEDIA USAGE AT WORK
Statistics

How much of work time taken up per person in an average working day?

+120mins 9.4% +120mins
60-120 mins 9.6%
30-60 mins 12.3%
15-30 mins 17.2%
1-15 mins 18.7%
none 32.8%

This equates to a cost per employee per year in wasted wages of:

None 32.8% none
1-15 mins 18.7% up to £1,019.50
15-30 mins 17.2% up to £2,039
30-60 mins 12.3% up to £4,078
60-120 mins 9.6% up to £8,156
+120 mins 9.4% over £8,156

When employees use social media at work

40% intermittently throughout the day
11.6% during toilet breaks
4.5% other
41.4% during lunch
22.3% at the end of the day
6.4% when first arriving at the office

Why employees use social media at work

34% use social media while at work to take a mental break from their job
27% to connect with friends and family while at work
24% to make or support professional connections
17% to build or strengthen personal relationships with coworkers
20% to get information that helps them solve problems at work
17% to learn about someone they work with
12% to ask work-related questions of people outside their organization
12% to ask such questions of people inside their organization

Who is using it most:

25 mins women
39 mins men

Employers with and without social media policies

39% with
61% without

Impact of having a social media policy

Workers whose companies have an at-work social media policy say they use social media while on the job to:

30% take a break from work, compared with 40% of workers whose employers do not have such policies.
20% stay connected to family and friends while on the job, compared with 35% of workers whose social media use is not regulated at work.
Only 16% get information that's helpful to their job, compared with 25% of those whose workplaces have no such regulations

How employees view their use of social media

56% believe social media ultimately helps their job performance
22% believe that it mostly hurts
16% feel that it doesn't have much impact either way
6% see both the benefits and the drawbacks

The Cost of Employee Social Media

Averaged this is a cost of £2,592.60 per employee per year.

Multiplied by 30,645,000 employees in the UK, this is an annual cost to employers of £79,450,227,000 i.e. nearly £80 billion.

Factoring in lost productivity, this would cost even more.
8.13% of the working week is being lost on average. 8.13% of GDP is £185,884,320,000 i.e. nearly £186 billion in GDP lost every year to the UK.

Source/notes:

The cost per employee per year is based on a 2012 government report (NICE's Work-place Health report - Local government briefing [LG21]).
The NICE recommendations for local authorities and partner organisations on improving workplace health, using an average hourly rate of pay of £17.00 (£15.05 in 2012 increased by the increase in average weekly pay from 2012 to 2017), a national average week of 39.1 hours, a 5 day week and a 48 week working year.
30,645,000 people employed in the UK in 2016.
£2,286,400,000,000 GDP for UK in 2015.

column3: in which country has the first soil carbon project been launched?
column5: Kenya
column13:
A 4°C WARMER WORLD
We Can and Must Avoid It

Climate change is happening — now. Turn Down the Heat, a report on the latest climate science prepared for the World Bank, notes we're on a path to a 4°C Celsius warmer world by the end of this century. The social, environmental, and economic consequences could be devastating. Every region of the world would be affected, but the poor and most vulnerable will suffer the most. This is not inevitable, however — with sustained policy action and climate-smart solutions, warming can still be held below +2°C.

Human impact on the climate system is unequivocal.

Ice Age → Pre-industrial Times (around 1750)

Since the last Ice Age to pre-industrial times (thousands of years ago):

The global mean temperature rose by 4–5°C.

The same warming is taking place from pre-industrial times to 2100 (only about 300 years).

Pre-industrial Times (around 1750) → Today

Sea levels have risen 15–20 cm.

The global mean temperature is 0.8°C higher.

Ice sheets in both Greenland and the Antarctic are losing mass at an accelerating rate.

Since 2002, 13 extreme weather events like floods, heatwaves, and hurricanes have been linked to man-made change.

Today → Future

If present emission trends continue, the world is on a path to a 4°C warmer world.

Likely sea level rise: 0.5–1 meter

By 2100:
4° warmer

What could a 4°C warmer world look like?
Warming is likely to have more severe impacts on the tropics.

(Map labels / callouts:)
There would be larger impacts on agriculture and ecosystems.
Drought and aridity would likely increase in tropical developing countries.
Flooding and rising sea-level hotspots
Sea level rise is likely to be 15–20% higher in the tropics than the global mean.

Corals Driven to Extinction
Current
0.8°C warmer — Threatened with increased bleaching
2°C warmer — Growth slows down
3°C warmer — Begin to dissolve
4°C warmer — Extinction possible

Amazon Rainforest Dieback
Current
0.8°C warmer — Home to rich biodiversity
2°C warmer — Forest fires could double
3°C warmer — Large sections at risk of dieback
4°C warmer — Forests could turn into savannah in places

Water Resources Threatened
2°C warmer — Mean annual runoff decreases by >20% in the Mediterranean, Southern Africa, Central America, and parts of South America and Australia
4°C warmer — Major decreases in river flows in the Nile, Amu Darya, Indus, Ganges, Amazon, and Murray-Darling Basins
All above changes likely to double

What can we do?

The steps we take today can help countries transition to low carbon and climate resilient growth paths for the future.

WE MUST:
Make economic growth green and inclusive
Scale up affordable technology solutions
Invest in climate-smart infrastructure

Increasing finances to fight climate change

World Bank lending in 2012:
$7.1B
Mitigation

$4.6B
Adaptation

Making development climate-smart

Kenya
First soil carbon project
Higher yields, greater resilience, and lower emissions on 450,000 hectares

Nigeria

column3: Which two platforms are good for B2B companies?
column5: LinkedIn and Facebook.
column13:
Social Media Platform
Cheat Sheet

Pinterest
Key differentiating trait:
More of a niche platform, despite very large usage. Great for businesses that involve planning, design, creation, making. Heavily female audience.

Best for:
Link clicks

Resource and expertise level needed:
High

Good for these business types:
Restaurants
Interior design
Wedding venues

Twitter
Key differentiating trait:
Fast-moving, real-time, news-driven platform. Strong customer service element, be prepared to engage and respond to customers here.

Best for:
Link clicks, engagement, and foot traffic

Resource and expertise level needed:
Low

Good for these business types:
Retail
Nonprofits
Newspapers & media outlets

LinkedIn
Key differentiating trait:
Unlike almost every other platform, which is designed for the user to focus on their personal interests, this is a profession-driven platform.

Best for:
Link clicks, event registrations

Resource and expertise level needed:
Medium

Good for these business types:
Luxury goods & services
Software providers
B2B Companies

Instagram
Key differentiating trait:
There are very few ways to link from Instagram, so this is a brand-building platform. You need to have lots of high-quality images and videos to support this platform.

Best for:
Foot traffic, word-of-mouth awareness, engagement

Resource and expertise level needed:
Medium to high

Good for these business types:
Bakeries & Coffee Shops
Travel agencies
Art museums

Facebook
Key differentiating trait:
This is the platform, but it doesn't mean you need to be there. With the recent challenges the platform has faced, some users are choosing to spend their social media time elsewhere (like Instagram, which is also a Facebook platform). That being said, there's still no better place to find and engage customers, donors, or others.

Best for:
Link clicks, word-of-mouth awareness, event registrations

Resource and expertise level needed:
Low

Good for these business types:
Local Brick & Mortar Stores
Nonprofits
B2B Companies

Constant Contact
