column3: Who should you avoid close contact with?
column5: People who have flu-like symptoms.
column13:
COVID-19
Coronavirus Disease 2019

BE AWARE.

COVID-19 is a new respiratory illness that was first discovered in Wuhan, China. It is transmitted from person to person.

SYMPTOMS*

COMMON SYMPTOMS
RUNNY NOSE
COUGH
SORE THROAT

SEVERE CASES
HIGH FEVER
PNEUMONIA
SEVERE RESPIRATORY DISEASE

*Symptoms may appear 1 to 12 days following exposure to the virus

Who is most at risk of becoming seriously ill?
• People over age 60
• People with pre-existing conditions such as diabetes and heart disease

How is it transmitted?
• Through close contact with an infected person
• By an infected person coughing or sneezing
• By touching contaminated objects or surfaces and then touching your mouth, nose or eyes

Currently there is no vaccine or specific treatment. We can only treat the symptoms.
Serious cases may require oxygen and ventilatory support.

PREPARE.

Make sure you get your information from a reliable source.

Wash your hands regularly with soap and water or use an alcohol-based gel.

Cover your mouth with the inside of your elbow when you cough or sneeze or use a tissue and dispose of used tissue immediately and wash your hands.

ACT.

1M
Avoid close contact with people who have flu-like symptoms.

Avoid touching your eyes, nose or mouth with unwashed hands.

Avoid sharing cups, plates or other personal items and disinfect all surfaces that are touched frequently.

If you have traveled to areas where COVID-19 is circulating or have been in contact with someone who has it and you experience fever, cough or difficulty breathing, seek medical attention immediately. Do not self-medicate.

Note: Information may change when more is known about the disease.
February 2020.

PAHO
Pan American Health Organization

World Health Organization
Americas

BE AWARE. PREPARE. ACT.
www.paho.org/coronavirus

column3: Who is the current owner of the space stone?
column5: The Space Stone is housed in the Tesseract and is currently being sought by Thanos.
column13:
EVERYTHING
YOU NEED TO KNOW
BEFORE
WATCHING

AVENGERS
INFINITY WAR

THE INFINITY STONES

POWER STONE
- Amplifies the user's strength, energy and destructive capability
- Can be used to obliterate planets and power weapons
- Seen in Guardians of the Galaxy (as an orb)
- Currently: In Thanos' gauntlet (seeking)

SPACE STONE
- Allows teleportation and instantaneous travel across space
- Housed in the Tesseract
- Seen in: Captain America: The First Avenger, The Avengers, Thor: Ragnarok
- Currently: Sought by Thanos

REALITY STONE
- Alters reality itself; can transform matter
- Also called the Aether
- Seen in: Thor: The Dark World
- Currently: Sought by Thanos

SOUL STONE
- Connects to the essence of a being; can manipulate souls and life and death
- Highly mysterious; special rules
- Currently: Sought by Thanos

TIME STONE
- Manipulates time: loops, fast-forwards, rewinds, traps
- Housed in the Eye of Agamotto
- Seen in: Doctor Strange
- Currently: Sought by Thanos

MIND STONE
- Controls minds and can grant sentience
- Housed in Loki's scepter, later embedded in Vision's forehead
- Seen in: The Avengers, Avengers: Age of Ultron, Captain America: Civil War
- Currently: Sought by Thanos

[Central wheel with segments for each stone lists example uses, appearances and guardians. Each stone segment lists the films and characters most closely associated with that stone]

- Others (various side characters and aliens shown in small icons)

THE "FALSE" GAUNTLET
- A prop gauntlet went on display that isn't the final screen-used version
- It was much larger and more ornate than the gauntlet in the film
- This prop was used for promotional displays and photo-ops
- The real gauntlet used on set is more compact and designed to match Thanos' proportions

UNIVERSES & HOLLYWOOD

MARVEL CINEMATIC UNIVERSE
- The MCU is the most successful shared cinematic universe
- Launched by Marvel Studios in 2008 with Iron Man
- Films interconnect and characters cross over into ensemble movies such as The Avengers
- Marvel retained film rights to many characters but shared some rights with other studios; this has shaped who appears where
- Marvel Studios controls production of MCU movies while distribution and certain character rights involve other studios in some cases

DC EXTENDED UNIVERSE
- DC Entertainment and Warner Bros. created a shared universe for DC Comics characters beginning in the 2010s
- Includes films like Man of Steel, Batman v Superman, Wonder Woman, Justice League
- DC's approach often has a darker tone compared to the MCU

20TH CENTURY FOX
- Fox held film rights to the X-Men and Fantastic Four for many years, producing their own separate film series
- Because of this, those characters did not appear in the MCU films produced by Marvel Studios

SONY PICTURES
- Sony Pictures owns the film rights to Spider-Man and related characters
- A deal with Marvel Studios allowed Spider-Man to appear in the MCU while Sony retains distribution of standalone Spider-Man films

(Various studio logos accompany these paragraphs: MARVEL STUDIOS, DC, 20TH CENTURY FOX, SONY PICTURES)

- Small callouts list key facts: who created certain artifacts (Tesseract as Space Stone), where a stone was last seen (e.g., Aether/Reality Stone with the Collector), and which characters have wielded or been influenced by stones

- The Infinity Stones predate the universe and each embodies an aspect of existence
- Collecting all six allows the user to reshape reality
- Thanos seeks all six to fulfill his plan

There are THREe phases of the MCU so far

Universes & Hollywood

Marvel Cinematic Universe
The idea of a shared universe was brought in by Marvel Studios after they saw the success of the Avengers comics. Marvel Studios started the MCU in 2008 with Iron Man and have slowly expanded the universe with other interconnected films. Key movies introduced the Infinity Stones which are central to Avengers: Infinity War.

DC Extended Universe
DC Entertainment and Warner Bros. Pictures created their own shared cinematic universe starting with Man of Steel. Their films are loosely connected, often darker in tone, and the shared universe has been built with different directorial visions.

20th Century Fox
Although they announced plans to bring their characters into a cohesive universe, the X-Men and Fantastic Four properties at 20th Century Fox largely remained in separate continuities for a long time. The Fox movies often existed independently of Marvel Studios' continuity.

Sony Pictures
Sony still controls the film rights to Spider-Man and certain related characters. A deal with Marvel Studios allowed Spider-Man to appear in the MCU while Sony retains distribution rights to standalone Spider-Man films.

"The 'false' gauntlet"
A replica glove used in the movie to cover Thanos' hand during filming. The stones were added in post-production. The real gauntlet used for motion capture differs slightly from the final product seen on screen.

The War on Grad School

An educated population is necessary for a country to remain competitive in the global marketplace, but often personal and societal decisions make attending grad school less desirable.

Who's Going to Grad School?

In fall 2014,
1.8 MILLION graduate students were enrolled in master’s, graduate certificate, or doctoral programs in the U.S.

Over 40% of college freshman intend to pursue a master’s degree

(visual chart breakdown labels)

There’s no doubt an education is necessary, yet when it comes to grad school, for some the cons outweigh the pros.

WHO’S FIGHTING THE WAR ON GRAD SCHOOL? // POLITICAL REFORMS

Tax Reform Act of 1986
Payments for teaching and research assistantships were made taxable.

Technical and Miscellaneous Revenue Act of 1988
Technical and miscellaneous revenue act changes removed the tax-free treatment of many graduate student benefits.

College Cost Reduction and Access Act of 2007
Decrease in interest rates for undergraduate loans
No change in interest rates for grad students

Budget Control Act of 2011
Graduate students lost eligibility for some subsidized loan benefits and saw cuts to research funding.

Bipartisan Student Loan Certainty Act of 2013
Graduate students saw a hike in interest rates tied to the market.

2016 Tax Reform
Originally proposed taxing the income of graduate students who receive tuition waivers as compensation.
Proposed changes gained attention but were not enacted into law in final tax reform package.

ARE THE CRITICS RIGHT?

Reasons not to pursue a graduate education
Reasons not to pursue a graduate education include concerns about debt, opportunity cost, and personal sacrifices.

(Universe sidebar header)
Universes & Hollywood

MARVEL CINEMATIC UNIVERSE
A single shared film universe from Marvel Studios and Disney featuring heroes and villains from Marvel Comics. The MCU is produced in Phases. Phase One introduced the core team; Phase Two expanded the universe and characters; Phase Three builds toward major crossover events like Avengers: Infinity War.

DC EXTENDED UNIVERSE
DC Entertainment and Warner Bros. have their own interconnected film universe including Man of Steel, Batman v Superman, Suicide Squad and Justice League. Despite both being comic-book film universes, the DCEU is a separate continuity from the MCU.

20th CENTURY FOX
Although Fox produced films based on Marvel properties — including the X-Men and Fantastic Four — those films are part of Fox's continuity and not the Marvel Cinematic Universe.

SONY PICTURES
Sony Pictures produces Spider-Man films and holds the live-action film rights to the character. Marvel Studios and Sony collaborated on Spider-Man: Homecoming, allowing the web-slinger to appear within the MCU continuity while Sony retains distribution rights for standalone Spider-Man films.

Social Media Platform
Cheat Sheet

Pinterest
Key differentiating trait:
More of a niche platform, despite very large usage. Great for businesses that involve planning, design, creation, making. Heavily female audience.

Best for:
Link clicks

Resource and expertise level needed:
High

Good for these business types:
Restaurants
Interior design
Wedding venues

Twitter
Key differentiating trait:
Fast-moving, real-time, news-driven platform. Strong customer service element, be prepared to engage and respond to customers here.

Best for:
Link clicks, engagement, and foot traffic

Resource and expertise level needed:
Low

Good for these business types:
Retail
Nonprofits
Newspapers & media outlets

LinkedIn
Key differentiating trait:
Unlike almost every other platform, which is designed for the user to focus on their personal interests, this is a profession-driven platform.

Best for:
Link clicks, event registrations

Resource and expertise level needed:
Medium

Good for these business types:
Luxury goods & services
Software providers
B2B Companies

Instagram
Key differentiating trait:
There are very few ways to link from Instagram, so this is a brand-building platform. You need to have lots of high-quality images and videos to support this platform.

Best for:
Foot traffic, word-of-mouth awareness, engagement

Medium to high

Bakeries & Coffee Shops

Link clicks, word-of-mouth awareness, event registrations

Low

column3: What is the third phase of Love?
column5: Attachment
column13:
M

The
SCIENCE
of
LOVE

Love at First Sight
You see someone.
Within milliseconds, your medial prefrontal cortex tells you they're attractive.

Chemical Romance
Hormones and neurochemicals from your brain flood your body.
Love comes in THREE stages, which can overlap.

1
SEXUAL DESIRE
Possible without attachment or infatuation
Can rise and fall during long-term relationships
Testosterone increases in both men and women
Estrogen increases in women

2
LIMERENCE
Infatuation
A primitive instinct
Characterized by an increase in dopamine, which motivates you to pursue a reward
Serotonin decreases, which can sometimes cause obsession
Testosterone decreases in men and increases in women
(this may help men focus sexual interest)

3
ATTACHMENT
Increases with time in healthy and sustainable relationships, while limerence dwindles
Oxytocin (the "cuddle hormone") increases over time in long-lasting relationships

Love Can Be Scary
If you see someone you find attractive, your sympathetic nervous system may have a fight-or-flight reaction, releasing adrenaline, norepinephrine and cortisol.
Adrenaline and norepinephrine make your heart race.

Broken Heart?
Losing a loved one or a love interest can also cause a stress response in the body.
For older people, disruption in routine can cause depression.

Get the Nerve Up
Your vagus nerve runs from your brain to your stomach.
Your fight-or-flight stress response stimulates this nerve, which is why your stomach does somersaults when you see a person you find attractive.

Sources:

https://sitn.hms.harvard.edu/flash/2017/love-actually-science-behind-lust-attraction-companionship/

column3: Which color does 'K' represent in CMYK?
column5: K represents Black.
column13:
COLOR

Profiles & Printing Explained!

What's the Difference?
CMYK, RGB, and Pantone are industry standards for color profiles. How do they compare and what are their uses? Find out now!

CMYK

CMYK is the standard color profile for printing. Typically, your printer uses CYAN, MAGENTA, YELLOW, and BLACK inks to print colored images. When all colors are mixed together, a rich black is produced.

CMYK USE

CMYK is used for printed materials. CMYK colors do not appear as bright as RGB (they are missing the added bonus of a light source). Brochures, business cards, posters, etc. may be printed in CMYK.

RGB

RGB refers to the three primary colors, RED, GREEN and BLUE. RGB colors use light to make their colors bright. If you mix them all together you would get pure white.

RGB USE

RGB is the color profile used for digital screens and devices. Your screen mixes red, green, and blue light to produce the bright colors you see on your display. Websites, mobile apps, video, etc. are in RGB.

PANTONE

The Pantone Matching System (PMS) is used to correctly match materials with their corresponding RGB or CMYK colors. This insures that a color looks the same each time it is produced when matched to a Pantone swatch.

PANTONE®
247 C

PMS USE

Pantone Matching System can be used to accurately match colors for anything from fabrics and paints to t-shirts and printed materials.

What About Printing?
Knowing the differences between the color profiles can help you to understand how different printing processes work and how those processes will affect your completed project.

SPOT COLOR (OFFSET)

+ =

PANTONE®
369 C

PANTONE®
350 C

Spot color printing describes the process where specialized ink or specially blended ink is created to make a perfect match that corresponds to your desired color, such as a Pantone swatch.

If there is just one color to be printed, there will be a single plate, and a single run of the press. If there are two colors, there will be two plates and two runs, and so on. The colors are layered onto the paper one by one.

BEST FOR:
Spot color printing would be typically used for jobs which require no full color imagery, such as for business cards and other stationery, or in monotone (or duotone etc) literature such as black and white newspaper print.

If you want something printed that has a unique color, like a metallic or fluorescent, spot color printing will be needed.

FOUR COLOR (OFFSET)

+ + + =

C M Y K

4 Color process printing refers to the CMYK printing process. This means your artwork will be printed using only Cyan, Magenta, Yellow, and Black ink colors.

Just like spot color printing, each color is run through one at a time. The layering of each ink color produces a final product with a full color spectrum.

BEST FOR:
Four color process is the most common form of printing and is best for large batches, like 500 postcards. It produces clear, sharp prints.

* In some cases it may be possible to do a six-color or eight-color print. This type of process is more expensive, but will produce a wider range of colors.

DIGITAL PRINTING

Digital printing uses either toner or ink-jet technology. Digital printing might still use CMYK, but all colors are applied at once instead of with the "offset" roller method.

Although it has been considered in the past to not be as "crisp" as offset printing, technologies continue to improve and digital printing can produce some great results.

BEST FOR:
Digital printing is recommended for small batches, like 20 invitations or just one poster.

Because digital printing does not require the creation of printing plates, turn-around time is often much faster.

The Logo Co
Taking Design To New Heights

thelogocompany.net

column13:
Social Media & Social Change

Thanks to social media, individuals and organizations have been empowered to advance social change in education like never before. Check out how some of the pros are doing it, and brush up on a few ways you can help, too.

Stats on Supporting Social Change

73% of U.S. adults say being involved in social change is personally important to them.

Likelihood to engage in positive social change when economic conditions are bad:

60% Women
53% Men
69% Join Conversation
53% Start Conversation

People are more likely to join digital social change conversations than to start them.

Making Social Change

With their ties to social media, these organizations are making it a snap for millions of people to help their causes.

KICKSTARTER

Kickstarter's all-or-nothing funding method requires projects to reach their funding goals before they receive any money. This allows people to test how much support there is for an idea. Search the site for classroom needs and projects that can be supported.

DonorsChoose.org

Teachers post their classroom needs, everything from pencils to microscope slides, and people can donate online to whichever need they like. Once the project reaches its funding goal, the materials are delivered to the school. Photos of the project taking place are sent to donors.

PENCILS of PROMISE

How Social Media can be used for Social Change
Don't worry, one person can make a difference in the world. Here's how:

Email
Send individual, personal emails about your cause to folks, like legislators.

Blogging
Ask local weekly newspapers or on-topic blogs if you can write a guest post on your cause.
Begin your own blog for your cause, and become a resource for others.
Email a link to your post to legislators and the cause's PR people for more visibility.
Inspire others to get fired up about your cause and possibly donate.

Facebook & Twitter
Like the fan pages and accounts of causes you care about.
Like and follow legislators and other people interested in these causes.
When the causes post something you like, share it on your profile or retweet it.
Tag legislators and others in posts about your cause so they'll stay updated too.
Search through Facebook's causes app to connect with others interested in the same issue.

column13:
businessofanalyticsweekly.com | info@businessofanalyticsweekly.com | www.analyticsweek.com

Investigating
Data Scientists

Understanding their Skills, Roles and Team Makeup

A Study of 600+ Data Scientists*

Respondents provided self-assessment ratings about proficiency in 5 skill areas:

1. Business
2. Technology
3. Programming
4. Math
5. Statistics

Respondents provided information about their team makeup, job role, industry, satisfaction with work outcome, gender and more.

Most respondents were from B2B companies (80%), North America (64%). About 46% work for companies with less than 100 employees.

*Results shown are from respondents who answered the relevant question. Respondents were recruited using AnalyticsWeek newsletter, May 2015. Respondents could select more than one industry; therefore totals may exceed 100%.

Top 5 Industries Employing Data Scientists

1. Information Technology (26%)
2. Education / Science (14%)
3. Consulting (13%)
4. Financial Services (11%)
5. Healthcare & Medical (9%)

[bar chart of industries — labels include many industries such as Information Technology, Education/Science, Consulting, Financial Services, Healthcare & Medical, Government, Retail, Telecommunications, Energy, Manufacturing, Media/Entertainment, Transportation, Other]

Data Scientists are Highly Educated

Most data scientists hold graduate degrees (~67%)

Nearly half of data scientists hold a Master's degree: 46%

There was no difference between males and females in their educational attainment

[stacked bar chart showing Education levels by gender — Graduate degree, Master's, Bachelor's, High School / Other]

Data Science Skills

Top 10 Data Science Skills

1. Communication
2. Structured Data
3. Data Mining and Visualization Tools
4. Science / Scientific Method
5. Math
6. Project Management
7. Data Management
8. Statistics and Statistical Modeling
9. Product Design and Development
10. Business Development

[horizontal proficiency bars showing distribution of proficiency levels from No Proficiency to Expert for each skill]

Proficiency Levels Legend:
No Proficiency | Basic | Intermediate | Advanced | Expert

Skill Areas of Data Science

1. Subject Matter
- Business domain expertise
- Product knowledge
- Strategy & goals
- Research questions & interpretation

2. Tools & Technology
- Programming, Data Storage, Middleware, Tools
- Data engineering, ETL, productionization
- Visualization, dashboards, BI, reporting

3. Statistics
- Descriptive & inferential statistics
- Hypothesis testing, experimental design, causal inference
- Machine learning, predictive modeling, time series
- Statistical thinking & modeling

4. Data Management
- Data integration, data cleaning, data quality
- Metadata, governance, security and access

[diagram combining these skill areas]

Data Science Roles

Four Types of Data Scientists

"Researcher" is the most popular data science job role

[bar chart showing distribution of roles: Researcher, Developer, Creative, Business Manager]

Descriptions:
- Business Managers include leaders, business persons and entrepreneurs
- Developers include developers and engineers
- Creatives includes Jack of all trades, artists and hackers
- Researchers include scientists and statisticians

Data Scientists are not Created Equal

- Business Managers understand business (i.e., subject matter) but not the stats
- Developers understand technology and programming but not business and stats
- Creatives possess a basic knowledge in analytics
- Researchers are strong in statistics and math but weak in business and programming

[radar chart illustrating strengths of each role across Subject Matter, Technology, and Statistics]

Data Science is a Team Sport

Diverse Data Science Teams Perform Better

Researchers and Developers report better outcomes of analytics projects when they work with Business experts

[bar chart comparing project outcomes with and without Business experts for Researchers and Developers]

Business Managers report better outcomes of analytics projects when they work with Statistics experts

[bar chart comparing project outcomes with and without Statistics experts for Business Managers]

Bigger Teams, Bigger Outcomes

Data scientists who work with 4 or more people on their analytics projects report better outcomes compared to their counterparts who work with fewer people.

[bar chart comparing outcomes for teams of 1–3 people vs. 4+ people across Success metrics such as Project Success, Faster insights, Better adoption]

Getting Insights from your Data

The Scientific Method

1. Formulate a question or problem statement.
2. Generate a hypothesis that is testable.
3. Gather/Generate data through experimentation or observation.
4. Analyze data/Draw conclusions (e.g., test hypothesis).
5. Communicate results/Take action (e.g., change processes) based on conclusions. Refine hypotheses for further tests.

[flow diagram showing steps and feedback loop]

The scientific method is a way of investigating phenomena, acquiring new knowledge, or correcting and integrating previous knowledge.

Three Primary Skills of Data Scientists

Data scientists bring three primary skills to bear on problems.

[three-circle Venn diagram: Subject Matter, Technology, Statistics — overlap labeled Data Science]

1. Subject Matter expertise helps you know what questions you need to ask.
2. Technology expertise helps you access the data and process it quickly.
3. Statistics expertise helps you apply the right analytics to answer your questions.

How to Apply Data Science Skills to the Scientific Method

The scientific method helps data scientists organize their collective efforts to extract insight and value from data.

1. Formulate a question or problem statement — Subject Matter
2. Generate a hypothesis that is testable — Subject Matter + Statistics
3. Gather/Generate data — Technology
4. Analyze data/Draw conclusions — Statistics + Technology
5. Communicate results/Take action — Subject Matter + Technology

[matrix diagram mapping each step to the three skill areas]

Learn More about the Practice of Data Science

For more detailed information about the results contained in this infographic, check out these articles:

- Investigating Data Scientists, their Skills and Team Makeup http://bit.ly/1LQf0aY
- Getting Insights Using Data Science Skills and the Scientific Method http://bit.ly/1LQf0aZ
- Top Data Science Skills Report http://bit.ly/1LQf0aa
- Data Science Career Paths and the Practice of Data Science http://bit.ly/1LQf0ab
- Why Data Science Teams Report Better Work Outcomes http://bit.ly/1LQf0ac

Report Methodology and Details: http://bit.ly/DataScienceReportMethod

MARKET DRIVERS

WHAT ARE CONSTRAINTS TO INVESTMENT?

UNMET CUSTOMER NEEDS
Why should I pay for a toilet or latrine?
Why should I settle if I can’t afford the dream?
The poor are challenging customers who would rather make do with their current sanitation situation if they cannot find a desirable, affordable product in their local market.

WEAK SUPPLY CHAIN
How do I reach poor households?
Why should I prioritize sanitation in my portfolio?
The sanitation market is highly fragmented and uncoordinated, making it difficult to develop and deliver products that consumers are willing and able to buy. Enterprises with the scale to tackle these industry challenges do not currently see sanitation or the poor as priority markets; businesses that do focus on sanitation have limited reach and product differentiation.

DIFFICULT INVESTMENT CLIMATE
How can we improve the ease of doing business in the sanitation sector?
Why should we invest in this even riskier business?
Current sanitation policies are not focused on lifting barriers to market entry. Despite the existence of government sanitation promotion programs, sanitation enterprises do not have enough information about viable markets and lack sufficient access to finance.

KEY CHALLENGES

RECOMMENDATIONS

1 Stimulate Demand
- Show consumers that good sanitation is a smart economic decision
- Design sanitation solutions that meet poor customers’ needs and preferences
- Offer flexible financing options

2 Strengthen the Sanitation Supply Chain
- Build business capacity and market intelligence
- Encourage formation of strategic partnerships to develop stronger distribution channels to reach remote customers

3 Improve the Investment Climate
- Communicate clear policies that promote improved sanitation
- Facilitate private sector provision by clearly defining the role of the private sector in government strategy and policy

For more information, visit www.wsp.org/dpsp

column3: What kind of Cholesterol is not harmful to your body?
column5: High-density lipoprotein (HDL) — the "good" cholesterol.
column13:
YOUR HEALTH IN ONE DROP

Healthcare is confusing. It makes managing your health hard. With changing health plans, multiple & disconnected practitioners, and wanting to focus on prevention, we need to take responsibility for our own health.

THE POWER OF BLOOD DIAGNOSTICS

70% of data needed for accurate diagnosis and health management is in your blood. This data is critical for wellness and prevention, not just treating disease.

70%

GOOD NEWS:
Your blood cells regenerate every 120 days, so you can quickly measure significant improvement from lifestyle and nutritional changes.

120 days

CARDIOVASCULAR HEALTH

For 150,000 people each year, the first symptom of heart disease is death.

Approximately 41% of the people who experience a heart attack will die from it.

Blood diagnostic testing can provide greater knowledge about the risks for heart disease and other chronic illnesses by giving better insight into the following areas:

CHOLESTEROL

Not all cholesterol is dangerous. Some types, like High Density Cholesterol, can actually help clear your blood vessels.

HDL ("GOOD") CHOLESTEROL
FOODS THAT PROMOTE HDL PRODUCTION:

Oatmeal
Fish
Nuts

LDL ("BAD") CHOLESTEROL
ELEVATED BY:

Cheese
Fried Foods
High Glycemic Foods

HOWEVER, WE NEED TO LOOK BEYOND HDL/LDL LEVELS.

50% of heart attack patients have normal HDL and LDL levels. Measuring your LDL sub-particles such as Apo B and Lp(a) gives you a more accurate assessment of your risks.

50%

TRIGLYCERIDES

A form of fat in the body linked with heart disease and diabetes.

ACQUIRED BECAUSE OF:

Obesity
Cigarette Smoking
High-Carb Diet
Alcohol

Apo-B, which is not part of a standard cholesterol test, is responsible for linking bad cholesterol to blood vessels, and high levels can be a better predictor of risk of heart attack or stroke.

INFLAMMATION

A major contributor to many chronic diseases such as heart disease, cancer, and dementia is inflammation. Recent studies have even shown that inflammation negatively impacts exercise capacity. This can be measured in your blood by the High-Sensitivity C-Reactive Protein (hs-CRP) marker.

ANTI-INFLAMMATORY FOODS

Salmon
Blueberries
Oranges
Green Tea
Extra Virgin Olive Oil
Papayas
Sweet Potatos
Broccoli

THYROID

More than 1 in 10 Americans have Thyroid Disease, commonly causing fatigue and weight gain. Most are undiagnosed.

Hypothyroidism, a condition in which the thyroid produces too little thyroid hormone, causing the body to burn energy slower, can:

Cause weight gain and increase risk for heart disease.

Increase cold intolerance and fatigue.

Increase cholesterol levels due to inhibition of the body's ability to metabolize energy.

VITAMIN D

Because we are not outdoors and don't get as much sun as our ancestors, more than 75% of US adults have insufficient levels of Vitamin D. This has been associated with an increased risk of cardiovascular disease, cancer, and infection.

75%

Taking fish oil also aides in Vitamin D production.

Everyone has a different optimal level of Vitamin D; testing gives insight into how you achieve yours.

SMART BLOOD TEST

OVERALL HEALTH

TIME

Regular blood diagnostics can help you keep track of your overall health in a more meaningful way and, more importantly, identify potential health threats that standard testing may not find.

CONTROL OF DATA

Having a wellness team review your results and offer holistic guidelines on how to improve your health ensures you aren't just healthy today, but you are optimized for the future.

WellnessFX™

SOURCES: THEVAPTEST.COM | NIH.GOV | RIGHTDIAGNOSIS.COM | IHEART.ORG | JOHNSHOPKINSHEALTHALERTS.COM | ADVAMED.ORG

Created by COLUMN FIVE
column15: HDL (high-density lipoprotein) — the "good" cholesterol. It helps remove excess cholesterol from your arteries and is not harmful (higher HDL is linked to lower heart disease risk).

THE LIFESPAN OF STORAGE MEDIA

FOR AS LONG WE HAVE BEEN INVENTING WAYS TO CAPTURE AND PRESENT DATA,
we have been developing media on which to store it. With each new innovation, we've seen greater
convenience, increased storage capacity, and longer lifespans. But at some point, even with the utmost
care, media fails... leaving us with a useless piece of plastic, tape, or metal.

The Cloud, with its extensive network of server farms, leverages redundant storage media, ensuring that
in the event of hardware failure, the data lives on, stored safely in another part of the cloud. By way of
comparison, we've assembled some lifespan statistics for storage media throughout the digital ages.

column13:
WHY FREEZE-DRIED FOOD?

Originally produced for the U.S. Military and NASA, freeze-dried food has several advantages over canned or dehydrated foods.

LONG SHELF-LIFE

Uses two different types of packaging for freeze-dried food

The food can be stored at room temperature without refrigeration

EASY PREPARATION

Freeze-dried products can be rehydrated (reconstituted) very quickly and easily

All that is required is water. Although hot water is recommended for the fastest reconstitution time (8-10 minutes) and the best taste

freeze drying process

CONVENIENT STORAGE

98% of the water is removed from our foods, making them incredibly light, portable, and compact

3-month supply of food for a family of 5 can easily be stored under a twin-sized bed.

GOURMET QUALITY

it's food that not only you but your kids will want to eat as well

The food tastes like actual homemade meals, not something that came out of a bunker

NUTRITIOUS

The freeze dried process does not cause the same damage or toughening to the food as other processes do

HOT                               25 Years                              COOL

Can retain the same nutritional value, taste, texture and color for over 25 years when stored in a cool dry place

The Jerome Biblical Commentary 1990 // salvationarmyusa.org

THE BRUTAL ORIGINS

-- The tradition of Christmas is highly regarded as a celebration of the birth of Jesus Christ. Although popular belief states that Jesus was born on December 25th, The New Testament gives no specific date or year of his birth.

-- Roman pagans first introduced the holiday of "Saturnalia" which was a week-long period of lawlessness celebrated between December 17 - 25. During this period, Roman courts were closed, and Roman authorities stated that no one could be punished for breaking many of Rome's common laws.

-- The festival began when Roman authorities chose a random victim to represent "The Lord of Misrule." At the end of the festival on December 25th, the innocent man or woman would be brutally murdered. By doing so, Romans believed they were destroying the "forces of darkness."

-- In the 4th century CE, Christianity adopted the "Saturnalia" festival in attempts to convert large amounts of people to Christianity. The problem was - there was nothing intrinsically Christian about the festival. To fix this, Christian leaders claimed Saturnalia's final day, December 25th, was the official birth date of Jesus Christ.

...and Christmas was born.

column13:
Essay
War Literature

The Art of War

Author
Sun Tzu

Years Written
c. 475–221 BCE

Original Language
Chinese

CONTEXT

A Battle Won Before It Is Fought

Likely written during the violent and chaotic Warring States period of Chinese history (475–221 BCE), The Art of War offers a series of military strategies to wage war, involving such tactics as careful planning before battle. The strategies and tactics are analyzed and elaborated on by 13 commentators.

The Tao
Sun Tzu was influenced by Taoist philosophy, which emphasizes balance, virtuous choices, and harmony with nature.

Warring States Period
Sun Tzu lived during a turbulent period of military conflict among China's provinces.

Chinese War Literature
The Art of War was the first of the seven military classics in Chinese literature.

The Art of War by the Numbers

Main Ideas

Predecided Outcomes
Good resources, training, and strategy are the keys to victory.

Changing Tactics
War is unpredictable, a fact that good leaders use to their advantage.

Strengths & Weaknesses
Wise commanders know theirs and their enemies' strengths and weaknesses.

Deceit as Strategy
Fooling the enemy into making miscalculations wins battles.

A victorious army wins its victories before seeking battle.
Sun Tzu, Chapter 4

Author

SUN TZU
c. 400–301 BCE

An experienced professional tactician and strategist, Sun Tzu fought for the state of Wu during a time when China's states fought one another for primacy. He formulated his 13 topics of military strategy as an organized method of conducting war with the best possible outcome.

column3: Which social media site was invented in 2002?
column5: Friendster
column13:
friendster
THE HISTORY
of social media

Social media channels seem to be everywhere and with 135 minutes of our time spent on social media platforms every day, it's crazy to think that it's only been 20 years since the first social media site was made. Without any further ado, here we have the history of social media.

THE BEGINNING
Samuel Morse made the first step towards some resemblance of social media when he sent a telegraph from Washington D.C to Baltimore. Morse's first message read "What Have God Wrought?"

1844

1969

MILITARY'S FIRST SOCIAL MEDIA ATTEMPT
ARPAN (Advanced Research Projects Agency Network) wanted to connect Universities on a proto-internet. It worked great, if transmitting two letters was the goal. Unfortunately the letters "LO" weren't the complete "LOGIN" they had planned to send over.

START OF SOCIAL SITES
From 1997 to 2001, Six Degrees had a peak of about 1 million members who were able to create profiles and 'friend' each other.

1997
sixdegrees

1999

CREATIVITY AND STATUS BLOGS
LiveJournal was a platform where users could blog as a form of keeping friends updated with their lives. A format later used by Facebook for their 'status'.

A CHALLENGER EMERGES
Friendster, one of the first social media sites, is created and launched by Jonathan Abrams. Within the next year, they turned down $30 million dollars from Google, widely considered one of the biggest blunders of Silicon Valley.

2002
friendster

2003

LINK UP WITH BUSINESSES
LinkedIn gave users a place to post their resume and private message people with a business-orientated mind. It's now a very popular networking service for people to find work and for businesses to recruit. By 2004 the number of users on the site would reach 1 million, 3 years later they would reach 500 million users.

2004

GMAIL RECEIVED
Google creates its own email service, Gmail, in January. In the UK Gmail was branded as Google Mail due to a trademark dispute, until 2009 when they changed the branding back after the dispute was over.

2004

FACEBOOK BEGINS
In February 2004, Facebook was launched by Mark Zuckerberg, along with his Harvard roommates. The site was initially limited to Harvard students but by the following months and years, this membership would be worldwide reaching the number of users to around 2.2 billion in 14 years.

2005

BIRTH OF YOUTUBE
YouTube, a platform on which users can upload and share videos, was made public in February 2005. The first video to hit one million views was a cat video this year, thanks to a Nike advert featuring Ronaldinho. Facebook is now made available in the UK, Ireland, Canada, Australia and New Zealand on October 1st.

2006

MYSPACE IS BOOMING
Myspace became the most popular social network in the US during 2006! That's just 3 years after it was founded.
Twitter is launched publically on July 15th

750 TWEETS PER SECOND!
65 million tweets a day are being sent out on Twitter, that's around 750 tweets every second.

2010

2011

LAUNCH OF SNAPCHAT
Snapchat, an image messaging app, is launched in September after being created as an iOS-only app known as Picaboo in July. One of the founding members was later ousted before Snapchat is released.

SHORT VIDEOS, MEGA VIEWS
Vine, a video hosting service specialising in short videos, goes public in June. Twitter acquires Vine for $30 million in the October of that year.

2013
vine

2014

UK'S INSTA ADS
Instagram brings sponsored Ads to target the UK in September following their inclusion on the app in America.

LIFE OF A STARMAN
NASA's #YearInSpace started, giving Twitter followers updates on Scott Kelly, an astronaut who lived in space for a year!

2015

VR AND FAKE NEWS
Virtual Reality, live videos, and 360-degree videos reigned all video content on social media sites like Facebook, Twitter, and Instagram.
There was a crackdown on fake news throughout social media platforms like Facebook and Twitter

2018

After all of this, we now have a beautiful array of social media channels to pick and choose from; Facebook, Instagram, Twitter, Snapchat and more. We wonder what next advancement social media creators will think up. What do you think will happen in the future world of social media?

www.redpill.co

column13:
A 4°C WARMER WORLD
We Can and Must Avoid It

Climate change is happening — now. Turn Down the Heat, a report on the latest climate science prepared for the World Bank, notes we’re on a path to a 4°C warmer world by the end of this century. The social, environmental and economic consequences could be devastating. Every region of the world would be affected, but the poor and most vulnerable will suffer the most. This is not inevitable, however — with sustained policy action and climate-smart solutions, warming can still be held below 2°C.

Human impact on the climate system is unequivocal.

Ice Age  ➜  Pre-industrial Times (around 1750)

Since the last Ice Age to pre-industrial times (thousands of years ago):

The global mean temperature rose by 4–7°C.

The same warming is taking place from pre-industrial times to 2100 (only about 300 years).

Pre-industrial Times (around 1750)  ➜  Today

Sea levels have risen 15–20 cm.

The global mean temperature is 0.8°C higher.

Ice sheets in both Greenland and the Arctic are losing mass at an accelerating rate.

Since 2002, 13 extreme weather events like floods, heatwaves, and hurricanes have been linked to man-made change.

Today  ➜  Future

If present emission trends continue, the world is on a path to a 4°C warmer world.

Likely sea level rise: 0.5–1 meter

By 2100:
4° warmer

What could a 4°C warmer world look like?
Warming is likely to have more severe impacts on the tropics.

There would be larger impacts on agriculture and ecosystems.

Drought and aridity would likely increase in tropical developing countries.

Flooding and inland sea-level hotspots

Sea-level rise is likely to be 15–20% higher in the tropics than the global mean.

Corals Driven to Extinction
Current
0.8°C warmer — Threatened with increased bleaching
2°C warmer — Growth slows down
2.4°C warmer — Begin to dissolve
4°C warmer — Extinction possible

Amazon Rainforest Dieback
Current
0.8°C warmer — Home to rich biodiversity
2°C warmer — Forest fires could double
4°C warmer — Large sections transform to biomass woodlands

Water Resources Threatened
2°C warmer — Mean annual run-off decreases by 10–40% in the Danube, Mississippi, Amazon, and Murray-Darling Basin
All above changes likely to double

What can we do?
The steps we take today can help countries transition to low carbon and climate resilient growth paths for the future.

WE MUST:
- Make economic growth green and inclusive
- Scale up affordable technology solutions
- Invest in climate-smart infrastructure

Increasing finances to fight climate change

World Bank lending in 2012:
$7.1B
Mitigation

$4.6B
Adaptation

Making development climate–smart

Agriculture
Kenya
First soil carbon project
Higher yields, greater resilience, and lower emissions on 450,000 hectares

Nigeria
Green urban transport in Lagos
Bus Rapid Transport (BRT) system used by ~200,000 commuters/day

Energy
Bangladesh
Solar panels for off-grid homes
1.4 million+ low-income rural homes have electricity

Water
China
Integrating water resource management with pollution control
20 million+ people benefit

Policy
India
Development Policy Loan
Launching transformative actions in energy, water, industry, and tourism in Himachal Pradesh

Risk Analysis

Decision-making under uncertainty
Helping policy-makers make the right decisions on infrastructure in Ho Chi Minh

To learn more, go to climatechange.worldbank.org
Visit the World Bank on Facebook, and follow us on twitter @wbclimatechange

Source: Turn Down the Heat: Why a 4°C warmer world must be avoided

TAKEAWAYS

Keep it Simple: Positive Stories from Personal Friends Spark Purchase.
It's clear that friends and family members prove to be the most impactful drivers of purchase. While celebrities and professional bloggers may carry a huge network reach that is attractive to many brands, it is often the personal story from a trusted friend that influences action.

Facebook Friends Remain Critical.
While more networks like Pinterest and YouTube are increasing their reputation as trusted and reliable sources of information about products and services, Facebook still holds strong as a valuable source of information about products and services.

Develop Advocate Strategies Around Each Purchase Stage.
Product recommendations and reviews are leveraged at every stage of a consumer's purchase cycle. Consumers use different online platforms to satisfy their needs at each step — be it discovery, research or advocacy. It's important to seed conversations that are relevant to the consumer.

Mobilize Consumers. Influence. Maximize Social Impact.

column13:
YOUR HEALTH IN ONE DROP

Healthcare is confusing. It makes managing your health hard. With changing health plans, multiple & disconnected practitioners, and wanting to focus on prevention, we need to take responsibility for our own health.

THE POWER OF BLOOD DIAGNOSTICS

70% of data needed for accurate diagnosis and health management is in your blood. This data is critical for wellness and prevention, not just treating disease.

70%

GOOD NEWS:
Your blood cells regenerate every 120 days, so you can quickly measure significant improvement from lifestyle and nutritional changes.

120 days

CARDIOVASCULAR HEALTH

For 150,000 people each year, the first symptom of heart disease is death.

Approximately 41% of the people who experience a heart attack will die from it.

Blood diagnostic testing can provide greater knowledge about the risks for heart disease and other chronic illnesses by giving better insight into the following areas:

CHOLESTEROL

Not all cholesterol is dangerous. Some types, like High Density Cholesterol, can actually help clear your blood vessels.

HDL ("GOOD") CHOLESTEROL
FOODS THAT PROMOTE HDL PRODUCTION:

Oatmeal
Fish
Nuts

LDL ("BAD") CHOLESTEROL
ELEVATED BY:

Cheese
Fried Foods
High Glycemic Foods

HOWEVER, WE NEED TO LOOK BEYOND HDL/LDL LEVELS.

50% of heart attack patients have normal HDL and LDL levels. Measuring your LDL sub-particles such as Apo B and Lp(a), gives you a more accurate assessment of your risks.

50%

TRIGLYCERIDES

A form of fat in the body linked with heart disease and diabetes.

ACQUIRED BECAUSE OF:

Obesity
Cigarette Smoking
High-Carb Diet
Alcohol

Apo-B, which is not part of a standard cholesterol test, is responsible for linking bad cholesterol to blood vessels, and high levels can be a better predictor of risk of heart attack or stroke.

INFLAMMATION

A major contributor to many chronic diseases such as heart disease, cancer, and dementia is inflammation. Recent studies have even shown that inflammation negatively impacts exercise capacity. This can be measured in your blood by the High-Sensitivity C-Reactive Protein (hs-CRP) marker.

ANTI-INFLAMMATORY FOODS

Salmon
Blueberries
Oranges
Green Tea
Extra Virgin Olive Oil
Papayas
Sweet Potatos
Broccoli

THYROID

More than 1 in 10 Americans have Thyroid Disease, commonly causing fatigue and weight gain. Most are undiagnosed.

Hypothyroidism, a condition in which the thyroid produces too little thyroid hormone, causing the body to burn energy slower, can:

Cause weight gain and increase risk for heart disease.

Increase cold intolerance and fatigue.

Increase cholesterol levels due to inhibition of the body's ability to metabolize energy.

VITAMIN D

Because we are not outdoors and don't get as much sun as our ancestors, more than 75% of US adults have insufficient levels of Vitamin D. This has been associated with an increased risk of cardiovascular disease, cancer, and infection.

75%

Taking fish oil also aides in Vitamin D production.

Everyone has a different optimal level of Vitamin D: testing gives insight into how to achieve yours.

SMART BLOOD TEST

OVERALL HEALTH

TIME

Regular blood diagnostics can help you keep track of your overall health in a more meaningful way and, more importantly, identify potential health threats that standard testing may not find.

CONTROL OF DATA

Having a wellness team review your results and offer holistic guidelines on how to improve your health ensures you aren't just healthy today, but you are optimized for the future.

WellnessFX™

SOURCES: THEVAPTEST.COM | NIH.GOV | RIGHTDIAGNOSIS.COM | IHEART.ORG | JOHNSHOPKINSHEALTHALERTS.COM | ADVAMED.ORG

Created by COLUMN FIVE

column3: While setting up your table, what should you do 'for extra stability'?
column4: ["tape your paper to the desk", "tape your paper to the desk for extra stability"]
column5: Tape your paper to the desk.
column13:
HOW TO CREATE
BEAUTIFUL
CALLIGRAPHY

YOU WILL NEED...

a ruler or set square      permanent ink      nibbed drawing pen      gouache paint      paper      sticky tape

tissues      a couple of pencils      old cloths or towels      water      a flat-edged paintbrush      chisel-tip black marker

SETTING UP

your tools should be within easy reach

tape your paper to the desk for extra stability

work on a slanted desk or a table if possible

give yourself plenty of room to work

don't keep your left elbow next to your paper. If you knock it over, you'll spoil your work

make sure you're comfortable

PRACTICE MAKES PERFECT

tape two pencils together with sticky tape

hold your pencils at a 45° angle - the tips of your pencils are like the nib of your pen

keeping the pencils steady, start making some marks, try some zig-zags or curves

FILLING YOUR PEN

never dip your pen directly into the ink pot

draw your brush point horizontally across the nib or gouache paint across the pen's nib

you're ready to start some calligraphy!

BEFORE YOU BEGIN...

put on some old clothes - calligraphy can be messy!

make sure you're sitting comfortably - try not to slouch

cover your lap with some old towels or cloths - ink can stain your clothes

PREPARING YOUR PAPER

(drawings of lined paper with guide marks and letters "adgh" shown)

1 DRAW YOUR BASELINE
Find this line by aligning your ruler lengthways across the top of your paper. Then, with your pencil, draw a light line just above the bottom of the rule. This is your baseline.

2 CREATE A NIB LADDER
A nib ladder is a great guide to help you get consistent letter widths. First, mark the nib width along your ruler. Then use this measurement to mark across the page in a row of short vertical lines. Continue in this way until you have a diagonal line of 10 squares.

3 DRAW THE X-HEIGHT
A typical x-height for lowercase letters is about 4-5 nib heights. Using your ruler and a pencil, measure 4-5 nib heights and draw a horizontal line across your paper - this is your x-height.

4 DRAW THE ASCENDER & DESCENDER
From your x-height line, measure 4-5 nib heights above and below and draw two more lines. These are your ascender and descender lines.

5 DRAW THE CAP-HEIGHT
Cap-height is the height of the capitals in your calligraphy. It is a line between the ascender and x-height line.

WARNING
If you're drawing your nib holder with your pen, you will smudge your ink! To avoid smudges while you draw use clean disposable tissues. If you must rest your hand on the page, use your work glove or a shield to keep the ink from smudging. We recommend using a block chisel tip marker before using your nib pen until your pen gets more comfortable.

SOME LETTERS TO GET YOU STARTED
Hold your pen at a 30° angle for this alphabet

(a set of lowercase letter illustrations with stroke numbers and small guide notes; labels for some letters:)
draw the letter with a 30° angle
draw this letter with a 45° angle
draw this letter with a 60° angle
1 2 3 4 (stroke numbers shown next to each letter)

(letters shown:)
a c e
s i t
b d p
q g y
j u h
n m r
w v x

column13:
BUSINESS
ANALYTICS
vs
DATA
SCIENCE

In the age of big data, parsing unwieldy amounts of information can lead to world-changing innovation.
To understand this data, companies are hiring a variety of specialists, including business analysts and data scientists.

Who Are They?

BUSINESS ANALYSTS
Research and extract valuable information from structured and unstructured sources to explain historical, current, and future business performance; determine the best analytical models and approaches to present and explain solutions to business users.

DATA SCIENTISTS
Design, develop, and deploy algorithms through statistical programming that support business decision-making tools; manage large amounts of data; create visualizations to aid in understanding.

What Education Do They Have?

The majority of business analysts come from a variety of backgrounds including business and humanities, while data scientists tend to come from computer science, mathematics, and technology backgrounds.

BUSINESS ANALYSTS                       DATA SCIENTISTS
30%                                      28%
Bachelor's

56%                                      48%
Master's

11%                                      20%
Doctoral

Sample Undergraduate Majors

• Business Administration                   • Mathematics
• Information Technology                    • Computer Science
• Finance                                   • Information Science
• Political Science                         • Computer Software Engineering
• Anthropology                              • Technical Communications
• Economics                                 • Computer Information Systems
• History                                   • Statistics
• Psychology

What Skills Do They Have?

Both business analysts and data scientists are experts in using data to inform decisions, but they apply their skills in different ways, using the same or similar tools. The skills listed below describe someone who has earned a master's degree in the field.

Decision-Making
Business Analysts
• Instigate change by transforming business insights from interdisciplinary data analysis into tangible resources for business decision-making.

Data Scientists
• Discover the opportunity in the datasets by mining data and writing machine learning algorithms to support decision-making processes.

Applied Problem Solving
Business Analysts
• Define business problems and translate statistical analysis into data-driven business intelligence that improves business performance.

Data Analysis
Business Analysts

• Manage and organize large sets of multivariate data using methods like linear discriminant analysis and multinomial regression selection.

Analytical Models
Business Analysts

Data Scientists
• Experienced statistical programmers using languages like and tools such as SAS, SQL, R, SPSS, Python, and Knime.

Database Management
Business Analysts
• Define and align database requirements for various forms of data, coded and not coded, using tools like Teradata, Oracle, and Hadoop.

Data Scientists

column3: What kind of people are not suited for Coworking space?
column5: Doers, Thinkers, and Helpers.
column13:
What's your ideal workplace?

Many companies are just beginning to realize that workplace design directly impacts employee performance, yet research shows that 3 in 4 U.S. workers are not in optimal workplace environments.

Identify your workplace personality.

John Holland's Theory of Career Choice suggests that most people fit into six basic personality types in the workplace.

Enjoy leading, persuading, promoting and selling
Energetic, competitive and ambitious

Enjoy working with records, numbers or machines
Organized, orderly and precise

Enjoy working with tools and machines
Practical, mechanical and physical

Enjoy studying, solving problems and working with information
Analytical, intellectual and scientific

Enjoy creative activities such as art, writing and music
Expressive, nonconformist, original and independent

Enjoy activities that help people, such as teaching and healing
Friendly, supportive and trustworthy

Identify the ideal workplace.

The Open Office

This modern setup gathers employees in a large, open space. Without walls or cubicles, employees interact with each other frequently.

70% of U.S. workers go to offices with open floor plans.

Who should work here?
Helpers  Sellers  Doers

Allows privacy and ownership over workspace while still efficiently using square footage. (green check)
May decrease productivity due to constant noise and interruptions. (red x)

The Cellular Office

This traditional workspace is divided into individual offices, typically with doors for added privacy. Offices may be shared with more than one person.

76% of workers in cellular offices are very satisfied in their work environment.

Who should work here?
Organizers  Sellers  Artists

Offers more privacy, which is key for certain professions, while encouraging concentration. (green check)
Not the most efficient use of space. Closed doors result in less collaboration. (red x)

The Cubicle Setup

Between the open office design and the cellular office is the cubicle setup, which divides an open space into semi-private areas using medium to high walls.

90 ft² is the average cubicle size, half the size of a typical executive office.

Who should work here?
Thinkers  Organizers  Sellers

Allows privacy and ownership over workspace while still efficiently using square footage. (green check)
Decreases employee communication (e.g., cubicle offices), while noise levels and distractions remain high. (red x)

Telecommuting

Thanks to advances in communication technologies, this increasingly popular work setup allows employees to work remotely either full- or part-time.

50% of U.S. workers hold a job that allows some telework.

Who should work here?
Doers  Artists  Helpers

Saves thousands of dollars in overhead costs. Employees enjoy more flexibility, fewer interruptions and greater autonomy. (green check)
May result in isolation, lack of teamwork, and a loss of productivity, depending on the worker's home situation. (red x)

Coworking Space

Coworking spaces are shared office environments where remote workers can rent a workspace with access to printers, scanners and meeting space.

2x The number of coworking spaces worldwide doubles every year.

Who should work here?
Artists  Sellers  Organizers

Gives remote workers a chance to meet and network with a wide range of professionals. (green check)
Comes with its share of distractions and membership fees can be expensive versus working from home. (red x)

What's Next?

One size certainly does not fit all when it comes to workplace design. Progressive companies are experimenting with hot-desking (i.e. no assigned seating), movable desks and modular workspaces. Google has successfully adopted the idea of a hybrid workplace that features a wide range of spaces to suit various personalities and situations.

Improve your workplace.

Love your job, but not your workplace? Try these easy tips:

Craving some creative inspiration? Schedule a lunch meeting with a colleague, plan a brainstorm session, or opt for face-to-face conversations over email.

When you need more privacy, snag an open conference room or step outside for a breath of fresh air.

Sources:

10 steps TO BRILLIANT SOCIAL CUSTOMER SERVICE

The number of people engaging in online conversations with and about brands is increasing year-on-year. Monitoring these conversations has become as essential activity for any large organisation, but so far the results for Social Customer Service appear shaky.

THE RISE OF SOCIAL CUSTOMER SERVICE

Almost half of social media users have used social customer care1

Although this is particularly prominent in amongst younger users, it is by no means exclusive.

nearly 50% of 45-54 year-olds
over 1/3 of 55+ year-olds

The number of companies handling more than 25% of customer service enquiries via social media DOUBLED last year from 9% to 18%

30% of top brands now have DEDICATED Customer Service accounts on Twitter.2

WHY BOTHER?

71% of those who experience positive social customer care are likely to recommend that brand to others, compared to just 19% of customers that don't get a response3

90% of upset customers can be retained with great customer service4

THE CHALLENGES OF SOCIAL CUSTOMER SERVICE

only 36% of consumers that make customer service enquiries via social media report having their issue solved quickly and effectively.5

This is probably due to the fact that Marketing and PR departments remain the dominant force in social media, but are not equipped to deal with many customer enquiries.

% OF DEPARTMENTS INVOLVED IN SOCIAL MEDIA:6

Marketing 70%
PR 69%
Customer Service 19%

1 SELECT YOUR CHANNELS

Before diving straight in and responding, set up your search queries and bring in as much data as possible.

This will help you draw trends on what people are saying, what channels they're on and whether you have the resources to deal with it.

Nearly 1 in 3 social media users prefer to reach out to a brand for customer service through a social channel compared to the phone.7

2 SELECT THE RIGHT TOOLS

A sophisticated monitoring solution needs to combine social media listening with customer engagement and Social CRM.

21% of businesses offering a service do not use any tool to monitor social conversations
17.5% used paid tools.8

3 SET UP YOUR LISTENING PROGRAMME

Think carefully about what terms you will monitor for. Mentions of your brand, products with support related words, swear words, or words associated with known problems.

assistance help fail poor not working rubbish broken

95% of brand mentions are irrelevant to customer service

the challenge is to drill into the remaining 5%

4 EMPOWER YOUR STAFF

Empowering the social customer service team to make quick decisions and feel supported is essential. If you can't trust them, you have the wrong team.

“Social support team members must be trained to make decisions in the field and be the face of action... So give them the tools and authority to do whatever it takes to wow customers. Give them full authority to do what's needed, when needed, without approval.” 

CONNECT MULTIPLE CHANNELS

Conversations will jump between different channels. Organisations need to be able to track those communications across channels.

“Although social customer service tends to start off being public, we often resolve the problem offline, particularly if there's a serious or sensitive issue.” 
Ronan Gillen, eBay

5

6 PRIORITISATION

Decide how you will prioritise enquiries.

Will you prioritise influencers, urgent enquiries or will it be first come first serve?

(Example tweet)
Patrick Stewart
@AllWeDoIsWin
All I wanted to do was set up a new account with @TWCable_NYC but 36hrs later I've lost the will to live.

7 AUTOMATION

When applied intelligently, automation can be a great time saver. It can assign specific enquiries to certain team members and can direct enquiries from regular detractors or important customers straight to senior staff.

“The final stage is automation to make your listening platform more efficient. Sending certain types of question to a specific team member can really improve efficiencies.” 
Leon Chaddock, Sentiment

8 MEET CUSTOMER EXPECTATIONS

Without an effective social media listening process in place, brands cannot hope to live up to the demands of consumers in a world of real-time communications.

42% of users on Twitter expect a response to their customer service issue in under an hour.10

But average response times are:
2 minutes
357 minutes
819 minutes

9 ETIQUETTE

“companies that listen online are intruding on customers”

roughly 40% of consumers under 55

roughly 54% of consumers over 55

Jumping into conversations on social media can feel intrusive.

Brands should consider whether the customer wants their help before getting involved.

10 GAIN CUSTOMER INSIGHTS

The data gathered through social media monitoring can be incredibly useful. By categorising and sifting through data relating to past queries and complaints, brands can gain insights that simply aren't visible from day-to-day interactions.

91% of companies rank the ability to use social data to better understand the drivers of consumer purchase decisions as "high priority" or "very high priority"12

SENTIMENT

The new channel for SUCCESS

HOW DO CORONAVIRUS DIAGNOSTIC TESTS WORK?
(aka "Do I have / Did I have COVID-19?")

A
Molecular Testing
VIRUS-DERIVED

Nasal swab → SARS-CoV-2 → RNA → DNA

• DNA derived from patient samples is repeatedly copied and amplified

Light
(gene 1, gene 2, control plotted)

DNA Copies →

• Different colored dyes bind to genes for pieces of the virus.
• Another dye detects genes from related virus families.

Pros
• Early detection
• Quick processing (hours)
• Highly selective and specific

Cons
• Skilled personnel and equipment necessary
• Does not tell you if virus is still active
• Cannot detect those who've been infected and later recovered

B
Serological Testing
BLOOD-DERIVED

SARS-CoV-2 infects a patient

After 2 days:
Your spleen produces IgM, one of the first antibodies to attack coronavirus

9-11 days:
More tailored antibodies called IgG are produced

As the patient's immune system kicks in, the antibodies are measured using a technique called ELISA:

Antibody Concentration

Light

Pros
• Rapid test available at point-of-care
• Low cost
• Can be used to test large populations
• Reliably detects exposure

Cons
• Cannot detect if patient is contagious or infection is still present
• Prone to false negatives if patient has delayed immune response

column13:
HOW TO CREATE
BEAUTIFUL
CALLIGRAPHY

YOU WILL NEED...

a ruler or set square
permanent ink
nibbed drawing pen
gouache paint
paper
sticky tape

tissues
a couple of pencils
old cloths or towels
water
a flat-edged paintbrush
chisel-tip black marker

SETTING UP

your tools should be within easy reach

tape your paper to the desk for extra stability

work on a slanted desk or a table if possible

give yourself plenty of room to work

don't keep your left shoulder tense or you'll get poor spacing
if you rest your arm on the desk you will spoil your work

make sure you're comfortable

PRACTICE MAKES PERFECT

tape two pencils together with sticky tape

hold your pencils at a 45° angle - the tips of your pencils are like the nib of your pen

keeping the pencils steady, start making some marks, try some zig-zags or curves

FILLING YOUR PEN

never dip your pen directly into the ink pot

tilt your brush, scoop a horizontal strip of ink or gouache paint across the pen's nib

you're ready to start some calligraphy!

BEFORE YOU BEGIN...

put on some old clothes - calligraphy can be messy!

make sure you're sitting comfortably - try not to slouch

cover your lap with some old towels or cloths - ink can stain your clothes

PREPARING YOUR PAPER

(drawn guides image with letters a d g h and ruler marks)

1 DRAW YOUR BASELINE
Find this line by aligning your ruler lengthways across the top of your paper. Then, with your pencil, draw a line along the bottom edge of the ruler. This is your baseline.

2 CREATE A NIB LADDER
A nib ladder is a good way to get the size and spacing of your letters right. Using your pen nib, mark off the nib width along the left-hand edge of your paper, then repeat these nib widths down the page to make a ladder. Count out 10 squares.

3 DRAW THE X-HEIGHT
A typical italic typeface is about 4-5 nib heights for the x-height. Use your ruler to draw a horizontal line 4-5 nibs above the baseline. This will be the height of most lowercase letters.

4 DRAW THE ASCENDER & DESCENDER
From your x-height, measure 4-5 nib heights above and below to draw the ascender and descender lines. These show how far letters like b, d or g should extend.

5 DRAW THE CAP-HEIGHT
Cap-height is the height of the capitals. It should be around the same height as the ascender line.

WARNING
If you're drawing with your nib ladder or your nib over a wet area, be careful not to drag your paper across wet ink. We recommend using a block chisel-tip marker to practice once you're comfortable.

SOME LETTERS TO GET YOU STARTED
Hold your pen at a 30° angle for this alphabet

(o) 1 2 3 4
(c) 1 2
(e) 1 2 3

(s) 1 2 3
(i/j) 1 2 3
(t) 1 2 3 4

(w) draw this letter with a 45° angle

(a) 1 2 3

Cheat Sheet

Pinterest

Key differentiating trait:
More of a niche platform, despite very large usage. Great for businesses that involve planning, design, creation, making. Heavily female audience.

Link clicks

Resource and expertise level needed:
High

Low

Retail
Nonprofits

Key differentiating trait:
Unlike almost every other platform, which is designed for the user to focus on their personal interests, this is a profession-driven platform.

Best for:
Link clicks, event registrations

Medium

Software providers
B2B Companies

column3: What was the only media for watching Team USA events live in 2008?
column5: Television.
column13:
COMCAST BUSINESS
Proud Partner of Team USA

As Team USA is preparing for the Olympic Winter Games 2018, it is important to realize how The Games have changed over the years for Team USA. From how they trained for the Olympic Winter Games 1924 - the first Olympic Winter Games - to how we watched and shared Team USA's success - it's a whole new world, a world driven by technology.

Explore our online timeline. You'll find facts and comparisons on how Team USA and The Games have changed through the years and get a glimpse into the technology behind them.

Welcome to:
Team USA at the Olympic Winter Games

THEN & NOW

ENHANCED TRAINING WITH TECHNOLOGY FOR TEAM USA

1924

Early Days for Team USA at the Olympic Winter Games

Inability to monitor vitals or receive feedback during training

Could not learn from the practice runs, which made coaching a guessing game

Difficult to access medical history and past injuries slowed down recovery times

NOW

Technology Helps Prevent Injuries and Speeds Recovery for Team USA

Skin patches monitor heart rate, body temperature and sweat composition during training

Coaches learn from live feeds to a mobile app

Quick access to past medical records shortens recovery time

TEAM USA ATHLETES PRACTICE SMARTER, NOT HARDER

1936

First Alpine Skiing Event for Team USA

Trained the old-fashioned way — skiing down mountains

Warm weather locations at a disadvantage, couldn't train year-round

No feel for the actual course beforehand

NOW

Team USA Can Now Simulate Ski Runs

Immersive video technology Team USA skiers emulate any course

Athletes get to know the course without leaving the training facility

Immediate feedback allows for improvement

CHANGING THE WAYS WE WATCH TEAM USA

1960

Team USA's First Olympic Winter Games Broadcast on TV

13 hours of coverage over 11 days*

15-minute selection of taped highlights aired from 11:15-11:30PM

All coverage was in black-and-white on TV

NOW

Broadcast TV Not the Only Way to Watch Team USA

1,500+ hours of multiplatform coverage over 18 days

Livestream all events including Team USA at the opening and closing ceremonies

10.8 million hours of The Games consumed

AFTER 30 YEARS, TEAM USA RETURNS TO SOUTH KOREA

1988

A Look at Technology the First Time Team USA Competed in South Korea

Low quality CRT "picture tube" TVs with a max screen size of 27"

Personal computers had found their way into only about 15 percent of U.S. households

Cell phones offered 60 minutes of talk time and 8 hours of power before needing to be charged

NOW

New Technologies as Team USA Returns to South Korea

Flat screen 4K Ultra HD Smart TVs now available up to 110"

80% of U.S. households contain at least one desktop or laptop computer, 23% have 3 or more*

Today's smartphones allow you to watch video in high definition

SMARTPHONES ARE CHANGING THE WAYS WE FOLLOW TEAM USA

2008

A New Device

Only 9% of Americans reported owning smartphones in 2008

Nearly impossible to have coverage of The Games while on-the-go

Television was the only way to watch live Team USA events

NOW

In The Palm of Everyone's Hand

Now 77% of Americans own smartphones

Livestream any Team USA event from any mobile device

Gain extra insight into the Team USA athletes

©2018 Comcast Corporation

column3: What came first, bristle trees or feathered trees?
column5: Feather trees came first — they became popular in the 1920s, while bristle (bristle-brush) trees were first made around 1930.
column13:
VISUAL HISTORY OF
Christmas Trees

The first Christmas tree was the centerpiece of holiday festivities in fifteenth century Latvia. Young men and women danced and sang around the tree before setting it on fire the last night of festivities. From those early traditions, to the first American tree in 1816, and into the present Christmas season, Christmas trees have been the focal point of holiday cheer. This timeline takes a look at some of the significant moments in the modern history of this Christmas icon.

Landmark trees
Decoration inventions
Christmas culture
Tree industry

1600
First decorated tree

1883
First artificial tree

Sears, Roebuck & Company began offering the first artificial Christmas trees – 33 limbs for $.50 and 55 limbs for $1.00.

"It's not good to cut down trees for mere decoration," says President Teddy Roosevelt in 1901, launching a century long debate on real vs. artificial trees.

1910

1910s Glass candleholders & tinsel trees

Small electric lights are already invented, but are too expensive and dangerous for trees.

1920

1923 National Christmas Tree

The same large evergreen has been decorated every year since 1923. In 2011, the tree fell in a windstorm and a new one was planted.

1920s Tree farms

Overharvesting leads to evergreen shortages, leading to...

The industry grows rapidly. By 2002, over 40 million trees are sold in the U.S. every Christmas.

1920s Feather trees

Imported from Germany, feather trees become popular in U.S. and range in size from 2 inches to 6 foot.

1930
Brush company manufactures first bristle trees.

1933 Rockefeller Center

The tallest Christmas tree ever at Rockefeller was a 30 foot spruce in 1999.

1940

1940s Tree flocking

The West Coast reinvents Christmas with flocking kits that can be applied with vacuum cleaners.

1946 Bubble lights

1955 Disneyland Main Street Tree

Sixty foot tall live tree until 2008, when they switched to an artificial one for environmental reasons.

1958
Aluminum trees are first non-green artificial trees.

1957 How The Grinch Stole Christmas

Two classics that remind everyone it's the spirit of giving, not a tree, that makes Christmas.

1965 A Charlie Brown Christmas

Charlie's negative portrayal of artificial trees causes dip in sales.

1966 White House Blue Room Tree

National Christmas Tree Association begins presenting the White House with a tree from the annual Grand Champion award-winning growers.

1970

1970-80s
Plastic trees lead to explosion of artificial trees as green alternative.

1980 Largest tree lit in Gubbio, Italy

8.5 kilometers of cable outlines a 650 meter tall tree.

1989 A Christmas Vacation

The Griswold Family's quest for the perfect tree is full of hilarious holiday disasters.

1990

1990s Fiber-optic trees

In 1992, 46% of homes used an artificial tree. The number jumped to 58% in 2004.

2000s War on Christmas

Politicians debate whether it's more politically correct to refer to Christmas trees as 'holiday' trees.

Meanwhile, sales continue to boom. Over 17.4 million artificial trees were sold in 2007.

2001 Lifelike polyethylene trees

New consumer technologies drive a new era of Christmas tree decorations.

2011

Today, artificial trees range from lifelike to glamorous, complete with spinning motors, multi-colored lights, and polyethylene plastics.
